bridge.title: Ragged Paged Attention: A High-Performance and Flexible LLM Inference Kernel for TPU
bridge.abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) deployment is increasingly shifting to cost-efficient accelerators like Google's Tensor Processing Units (TPUs), prioritizing both performance and total cost of ownership (TCO). However, existing LLM inference kernels and serving systems remain largely GPU-centric, and there is no well-established approach for efficiently mapping LLM workloads onto TPU architectures--particularly under the dynamic and ragged execution patterns common in modern serving. In this paper, we present Ragged Paged Attention (RPA), a high-performance and flexible attention kernel for TPUs, implemented using Pallas and Mosaic. RPA addresses these challenges through three key techniques: (1) fine-grained tiling to enable efficient dynamic slicing over ragged memory, (2) a custom software pipeline that fuses KV cache updates with attention computation, and (3) a distribution-aware compilation strategy that generates specialized kernels for decode, prefill, and mixed workloads. Evaluated on Llama 3 8B on TPU7x, RPA achieves up to 86% memory bandwidth utilization (MBU) in decode and 73% model FLOPs utilization (MFU) in prefill. Integrated as the primary TPU backend in vLLM and SGLang, RPA provides a production-grade foundation for efficient TPU inference and offers practical insights into kernel design.
bridge.why_now: TPUs are becoming a real second supply line for LLM serving, and this is the first well-documented account of how paged attention actually maps onto TPU memory hierarchies rather than GPU ones. It is already shipping as the primary TPU backend in vLLM and SGLang, so it is deployed infrastructure, not a proposal.
bridge.why_not_higher: The evaluation is a single model (Llama 3 8B) on a single accelerator generation (TPU7x) with no head-to-head GPU comparison, so the TCO argument that motivates the paper is asserted rather than measured.
bridge.what_would_raise_priority: A cost-per-token or throughput comparison against a well-tuned GPU serving stack at matched model and batch conditions, plus results across model sizes and MoE architectures.
bridge.one_sentence_episode_hook: Everyone writes LLM inference kernels for GPUs — this is what happens when you have to rebuild paged attention from scratch for a chip that hates ragged memory, and hit 86% of theoretical memory bandwidth anyway.

bridge.title: Less Experts, Faster Decoding: Cost-Aware Speculative Decoding for Mixture-of-Experts
bridge.abstract: Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models have become an important approach for scaling Large Language Models (LLMs), but their inference efficiency depends strongly on expert activation patterns. Speculative decoding (SD) accelerates autoregressive generation by verifying multiple draft tokens in parallel, yet existing draft selection strategies primarily optimize acceptance likelihood. In large-scale MoE models, however, selecting draft tokens also determines the union of experts activated during verification. We observe that confidence-driven SD can introduce \textit{expert scattering}: high-probability draft tokens may route to disjoint experts, increasing expert-weight memory traffic and reducing the speedup from speculation. Motivated by this observation, we revisit draft-tree selection under the non-uniform memory-cost structure of MoE inference. We propose \textsc{EcoSpec}, a cost-aware speculative decoding framework that incorporates predicted marginal expert activation cost into draft selection. With a lightweight expert predictor and a dynamic expert buffer, \textsc{EcoSpec} favors draft paths that preserve high acceptance likelihood while reusing experts already covered by the current verification set, without modifying the target-model verification rule. We evaluate \textsc{EcoSpec} on three large-scale MoE models, including DeepSeek-V3.1 (671B), Qwen3-235B-A22B, and GPT-OSS-120B, across reasoning, coding, question-answering, and dialogue benchmarks. \textsc{EcoSpec} consistently reduces active expert footprints and improves end-to-end decoding speed, achieving up to $1.62\times$ speedup. These results show that accounting for expert activation cost is important for efficient speculative decoding in large-scale MoE models.
bridge.why_now: Every frontier open-weight model is now sparse MoE, and speculative decoding is standard practice, yet nobody has been optimizing draft selection for the thing that actually dominates MoE decode cost — expert weight movement. EcoSpec names the failure mode (expert scattering) and shows it costs real speedup on DeepSeek-V3.1, Qwen3-235B, and GPT-OSS-120B.
bridge.why_not_higher: The 1.62x is an upper bound rather than a typical figure, and the abstract gives no hardware, batch regime, or expert-predictor overhead accounting, so it is unclear how much survives outside single-stream low-batch decode where expert reuse matters most.
bridge.what_would_raise_priority: A batch-size and hardware sweep showing the expert-locality win holds when expert weights are already resident or when batching naturally covers most experts anyway.
bridge.one_sentence_episode_hook: Speculative decoding has always asked 'which draft tokens will the model accept?' — this paper points out that in a 671B MoE, the better question is 'which draft tokens will make us drag fewer experts across the memory bus,' and picking the cheaper path instead of the likeliest one is worth up to 1.62x.

bridge.title: Efficient LLM Serving for Agentic Workflows: A Data Systems Perspective
bridge.abstract: Agentic workflows are composed of sequences of interdependent Large Language Model (LLM) calls, and they have become a dominant workload in modern AI systems. These workflows exhibit extensive redundancy from overlapping prompts and intermediate results due to speculative and parallel exploration. Existing LLM serving systems, such as vLLM, focus on optimizing individual inference calls and overlook cross-call dependencies, leading to significant inefficiencies. This paper rethinks LLM and agent serving from a data systems perspective and introduces Helium, a workflow-aware serving framework that models agentic workloads as query plans and treats LLM invocations as first-class operators. Helium integrates proactive caching and cache-aware scheduling to maximize reuse across prompts, KV states, and workflows. Through these techniques, Helium bridges classic query optimization principles with LLM serving, achieving up to 1.56x speedup over state-of-the-art agent serving systems on various workloads. Our results demonstrate that end-to-end optimization across workflows is essential for scalable and efficient LLM-based agents.
bridge.why_now: Agentic workflows are now a dominant serving workload, and this is a clean argument that per-call optimizers like vLLM leave large cross-call reuse on the table. The proposed fix — treat LLM calls as query-plan operators with proactive KV/prompt caching and cache-aware scheduling — is a genuine bridge between database query optimization and LLM serving.
bridge.why_not_higher: The abstract reads partly as a position paper: a single 1.56x headline over unnamed baselines on unspecified 'various workloads', with no model, hardware, or cache hit-rate detail, and no mention of released code.
downgrade_reasons: vague workload and hardware description
single modest headline speedup
baselines not named
bridge.what_would_raise_priority: Concrete numbers — models, hardware, cache hit rates, and named agent-serving baselines — plus an open-source Helium release.
bridge.one_sentence_episode_hook: Your agent framework is running the same prompt prefix a hundred times and nobody told the serving stack — what happens when you give LLM inference a query optimizer?

bridge.title: CacheWise: Understanding Workloads and Optimizing KVCache Management for Efficiently Serving LLM Coding Agents
bridge.abstract: Coding agents are a fast-growing LLM application, executing as long-running closed-loop sessions in which LLM generations alternate with external tool calls. Yet, unlike chat workloads, their serving behavior has not been studied extensively. We address this gap by collecting a dataset of real-world coding assistant traces. Our analysis shows that coding agent sessions repeatedly reuse large prefixes and create sustained KVCache pressure that conventional LLM serving policies handle poorly.   Based on our analysis, we present CacheWise, a KVCache management layer that improves KVCache reuse for coding agent workloads. CacheWise combines prefix-aware scheduling with reuse-aware eviction guided by lightweight predictions from tool call metadata. Implemented in vLLM and evaluated on the collected traces, CacheWise reduces KVCache evictions by up to 2-2.6x and improves total agent session completion time by up to 3.5x.
bridge.why_now: Coding agents are now one of the highest-volume LLM workloads in production, and this is one of the first real trace-based characterizations of how their long closed-loop sessions punish KV cache policies designed for chat. The mechanism — prefix-aware scheduling plus reuse-aware eviction predicted from tool call metadata — is exactly the memory lens, not a keyword coincidence.
bridge.why_not_higher: It is a single-system vLLM implementation evaluated on the authors' own collected traces, so the 2-2.6x eviction and 3.5x completion-time numbers depend on a dataset nobody else can yet reproduce against.
bridge.what_would_raise_priority: Public release of the coding-agent trace dataset plus an upstream vLLM path, which would turn this from a strong point result into a shared benchmark the serving community argues over.
bridge.one_sentence_episode_hook: Your coding agent replays the same giant prefix hundreds of times per session — and the serving stack keeps throwing it away, because every eviction policy in production was designed for a chat that never comes back.

bridge.title: Nautilus: An Auto-Scheduling Tensor Compiler for Efficient Tiled GPU Kernels
bridge.abstract: We present Nautilus, a novel tensor compiler that moves toward fully automated math-to-kernel optimization. Nautilus compiles a high-level algebraic specification of tensor operators into efficient tiled GPU kernels. Nautilus's successive lowering design allows high-level optimizations, expression rewrites, and tile optimizations to be jointly applied in a single end-to-end system. Nautilus presents a novel auto-scheduler that discovers sequences of high-level optimizations, while preserving the regular program structure needed by tile optimizers. Nautilus's auto-scheduler captures complex interactions and trade-offs in the high-level optimizations, including aggressive global transformations like advanced reduction fusion. Nautilus is the first end-to-end tensor compiler capable of starting from a math-like description of attention and automatically discovering FlashAttention-3-like kernels, offloading the entire burden of optimization from the programmer to the compiler. Across five transformer-based models and 150 evaluation configurations on NVIDIA GH200 and RTX 5090 GPUs, Nautilus achieves up to 23% higher throughput than state-of-the-art compilers on GH200 and up to 42% on RTX 5090, while matching or exceeding manually written cuDNN kernels on many long-sequence configurations.
bridge.why_now: A compiler that starts from a math-level description of attention and automatically lands on FlashAttention-3-like kernels is the first credible evidence that the hand-tuned-kernel era has an expiration date, and it is measured on current hardware (GH200, RTX 5090) against cuDNN and SOTA compilers. Every serving and training stack downstream of hand-written attention kernels has a stake in whether this generalizes.
bridge.why_not_higher: The memory angle is real but indirect: Nautilus reasons about tiling, reduction fusion, and on-chip locality rather than KV cache reuse, eviction, offload, or tiering, so it lands on the memory lens through the kernel's data-movement structure rather than through a storage mechanism. Peak speedups of 23-42% are meaningful but incremental against already-strong baselines, and cs.PL framing narrows the natural audience.
bridge.what_would_raise_priority: Evidence that the auto-scheduler discovers genuinely novel kernels rather than rediscovering known ones — for example paged or KV-cache-aware attention variants a human has not already written.
bridge.one_sentence_episode_hook: FlashAttention took human experts years of hardware-aware ingenuity to invent; Nautilus is a compiler that rediscovers it on its own, from nothing but the math — so what exactly is left for kernel engineers to do?

bridge.title: Xema: Efficient Diffusion Serving through Fine-Grained Memory Management and Auto-Configuration
bridge.abstract: Diffusion models are increasingly deployed as production visual-generation services, where serving high-resolution image and long video generation is often limited by GPU memory. Popular memory-saving techniques such as weight offloading, sharding, and VAE slicing are often not practical because they tend to introduce significant performance overhead. In this paper, we present Xema, a memory-efficient diffusion serving system that exploits predictable tensor lifetimes for trace-guided memory optimization. For each request template, Xema derives an offline memory trace to identify short memory-pressure intervals and applies memory mitigation only within these intervals and only by the amount needed to fit the target GPU budget. Xema further constructs a static memory layout for tensors with predictable lifetimes, reducing fragmentation-induced reserved memory and making offline memory reasoning reliable at runtime. Built on this memory optimization layer, Xema introduces an offline planner that jointly selects parallelism, concurrency, and memory control under GPU memory and SLO constraints. The selected plan is stored in a plan table and directly used by the online serving runtime. We implement Xema on production diffusion pipelines and evaluate it with Flux.2, CogVideoX-5B, and LTX-2. Compared with existing serving configurations, Xema improves SLO attainment by up to 3.7x and reduces planning cost from 6.3 hours to 197 seconds compared with grid search.
bridge.why_now: Diffusion serving for high-res image and long video is now GPU-memory-bound in production, and Xema attacks it exactly where the memory lens lives: offload scheduling, fragmentation-induced reserved memory, and static tensor layout derived from predictable lifetimes. It is the PagedAttention-style memory-layout argument transplanted from KV cache to diffusion activations, which is a genuinely new angle for this show.
bridge.why_not_higher: The memory work is real but the win depends on diffusion's static, template-shaped tensor lifetimes — the trace-guided planning premise breaks under dynamic autoregressive workloads, so the method itself does not transfer to LLM serving. Baselines are described only as 'existing serving configurations' with no named competing system, and there is no mention of released code.
bridge.what_would_raise_priority: A named head-to-head against a real serving stack baseline plus released code, or evidence that the static-layout/fragmentation reasoning extends to workloads without predictable tensor lifetimes.
bridge.one_sentence_episode_hook: Diffusion serving keeps hitting the GPU memory wall, and Xema's answer is that most of that wall is not real allocation at all — it's fragmentation and reserved slack, and if you know when every tensor lives and dies, you can offload only in the seconds that actually hurt.

bridge.title: SwiftCache: Efficient LLM Serving for Multi-turn Conversations with Heterogeneous KV Cache Sharing
bridge.abstract: Multi-turn conversation is a fundamental scenario in LLM applications, widely used in chatbots and AI agents. As the conversation evolves, historical tokens accumulate continuously. Existing systems cache their key-value (KV) pairs to avoid redundant computation. However, limited GPU memory (HBM) capacity often forces these KV caches to be offloaded to CPU memory or SSD, making KV cache reloads increasingly costly in terms of latency as the context grows. Meanwhile, the constrained HBM capacity also limits the maximum inference length, thereby restricting the number of turns that can be supported in a conversation.   To address these two challenges, we propose SwiftCache, a collaborative inference system that enables heterogeneous models to share underutilized GPU memory and NVLink bandwidth within a server. Specifically, models with low KV cache demand donate idle GPU memory to store the prefix cache of high-demand models, allowing cross-model KV cache sharing over NVLink and avoiding slow PCIe transfers. SwiftCache further reduces memory pressure by keeping only the KV cache of the currently active layer in local GPU memory, thereby enabling longer-context inference. Our experiments on real-world workloads show that SwiftCache reduces P99 time-to-first-token (TTFT) by up to 69% and extends maximum context length by up to 3.98x compared to vLLM and SGLang, with minimal interference to co-located models.
bridge.why_now: This is a memory-lens paper with no costume: the entire contribution is where KV cache lives and how it moves — donating idle HBM across co-located heterogeneous models, routing prefix cache over NVLink instead of PCIe, and keeping only the active layer's KV resident to stretch context. Multi-turn serving is exactly where KV reload latency now dominates TTFT, so the framing lands on a live production pain point.
bridge.why_not_higher: The mechanism assumes a fairly specific deployment: a single server with NVLink and multiple co-located models whose KV demands are conveniently anti-correlated. Evaluation is against vLLM and SGLang on unspecified real-world traces with no venue or artifact signal yet, and the 'minimal interference' claim to donor models is the load-bearing assumption that gets the least scrutiny in the abstract.
downgrade_reasons: narrow hardware assumption (NVLink, multi-model colocation)
no venue or artifact signal
donor-model interference claim under-evidenced in abstract
bridge.what_would_raise_priority: Released code plus an interference study showing donor-model latency under adversarial, correlated-demand traffic rather than favorable workload mixes.
bridge.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the model sitting idle next to yours could lend you its GPU memory — SwiftCache turns a server full of competing models into one shared KV cache, and buys 69% off P99 time-to-first-token for doing it.

bridge.title: SMEPilot: Characterizing and Optimizing LLM Inference with Scalable Matrix Extensions
bridge.abstract: Modern CPUs increasingly integrate matrix extensions, such as Arm Scalable Matrix Extension (SME), that provide high-throughput matrix execution within the CPU. For LLM inference, however, these units are not a universal replacement for conventional CPU cores: prefill, decode, attention, and KV-cache operations expose different arithmetic intensities, vector behavior, and layout requirements, while SME units and CPU cores still compete for shared memory bandwidth. This paper studies this mismatch through a roofline-based characterization of SME-enabled CPUs and uses the resulting model to guide operator-level execution choices. We present SMEPilot, an LLM inference engine that selects CPU-only, SME-only, or cooperative SME+CPU execution for each operator shape. SMEPilot partitions matrix work across SME and CPU cores at tile granularity, overlaps SME-suitable matrix stages with CPU-suitable vector stages in attention, and maintains layout state so packed tensor representations are reused rather than repeatedly rebuilt on critical paths. Across Llama-3.2-3B, Qwen3-4B, and Qwen3-30BA3B on phone, PC, and server platforms, SMEPilot improves end-to-end inference performance by up to 3.94$\times$.
bridge.why_now: Arm SME is landing in shipping silicon (Apple M4-class cores, upcoming Arm server/client parts) and this is one of the first careful characterizations showing the matrix unit is not a free win — it contends with the CPU cores for the same memory bandwidth. The roofline framing makes bandwidth, not FLOPs, the explicit decision variable for operator placement.
bridge.why_not_higher: SME-enabled hardware is still a narrow installed base, so the broad-audience payoff is more conceptual than immediately actionable, and the abstract gives no baseline detail or reproducibility signal behind the 3.94x headline.
downgrade_reasons: narrow hardware installed base (SME-only)
headline speedup lacks stated baseline strength
bridge.what_would_raise_priority: Open-source SMEPilot with a bandwidth-contention breakdown against a strong tuned CPU baseline (llama.cpp/oneDNN-class), so the roofline claims can be independently checked.
bridge.one_sentence_episode_hook: Your CPU grew a matrix unit — and the first thing it did was start fighting the rest of the CPU for memory bandwidth; SMEPilot uses a roofline model to decide, operator by operator, who gets to move the bytes.

bridge.title: Fleet: Hierarchical Task-based Abstraction for Megakernels on Multi-Die GPUs
bridge.abstract: Modern GPUs adopt chiplet-based designs with multiple private cache hierarchies, but current programming models (CUDA/HIP) expose a flat execution hierarchy that cannot express chiplet-level locality or synchronization. This mismatch leads to redundant memory traffic and poor cache utilization in memory-bound workloads such as LLM inference.   We present Fleet, a multi-level task model that maps computation to memory scopes. Fleet introduces Chiplet-tasks, a new abstraction that binds work and data to a chiplet and enables coordination through its shared L2 cache. Wavefront-level, CU-level, and device-level tasks align with existing abstractions, while Chiplet-tasks expose a previously unaddressed level of the hierarchy. Fleet is implemented as a persistent kernel runtime with per-chiplet scheduling, allowing workers within a chiplet to cooperatively execute tasks with coordinated cache reuse. On AMD Instinct MI350 with Qwen3-8B, Fleet achieves 1.3-1.5x lower decode latency than vLLM at batch sizes 1-8 through persistent kernel execution and per-chiplet scheduling. At larger batch sizes, cooperative weight tiling increases L2 hit rate (from 12% to 54% at batch size 32 and from 39% to 61% at batch size 64), reducing HBM traffic by up to 37% and delivering 1.27-1.30x speedup over a chiplet-unaware megakernel baseline.
bridge.why_now: Chiplet GPUs are now the default at the high end (MI300/MI350, and NVIDIA moving the same direction), yet CUDA/HIP still expose a flat hierarchy that hides the one cache level that decides decode performance. Fleet names that gap and pays for it with real counters: L2 hit rate 12%→54%, HBM traffic down 37%, 1.3-1.5x lower decode latency than vLLM on real MI350 silicon.
bridge.why_not_higher: Evaluation is one model (Qwen3-8B) on one vendor's hardware, and the win requires adopting a new task abstraction and persistent-kernel runtime rather than dropping into an existing serving stack.
bridge.what_would_raise_priority: Results across multiple model sizes and a second chiplet architecture, or evidence that the Chiplet-task abstraction lands in a mainstream runtime like vLLM or HIP itself.
bridge.one_sentence_episode_hook: Your GPU is secretly several GPUs glued together, and the programming model has been lying to you about it — Fleet exposes the chiplet, and LLM decode gets 1.5x faster because the weights finally stop making round trips to HBM.

bridge.title: ObjectCache: Layerwise Object-Storage Retrieval for KV Cache Reuse
bridge.abstract: Prefix KV caching has become a key mechanism in LLM serving: it reduces time to first token (TTFT) by avoiding redundant computation across requests that share a prefix (i.e., the system prompt). However, the accumulated KV cache is often larger than what GPU memory and local DRAM can hold. To preserve latency, current systems keep the KV cache in remote DRAM pools, increasing serving-cluster size and cost. In this paper, we explore a different approach: storing the KV cache in S3-compatible object storage so that capacity is no longer the constraint, while minimizing the impact on TTFT. We propose ObjectCache, which co-designs the storage protocol and transfer schedule so that the storage server delivers KV cache data in the order the GPU consumes it, overlapping data transfer with compute across concurrent requests. We prototype ObjectCache on a 100 Gbps RoCE cluster with NIXL (an inference library that abstracts storage and memory), Ceph RGW (an Object Gateway for clusters), and DAOS (an open source storage system). For 64K contexts, common in today's systems, ObjectCache adds only 5.6\% latency over local DRAM; for 4K contexts, where less compute is available to mask transfer, ObjectCache adds 56--75\,ms over the optimal local layerwise baseline. Under shared bandwidth caps, our scheduler reduces added TTFT by 1.2--1.8x compared with equal bandwidth sharing.
bridge.why_now: KV cache capacity is the binding constraint in production LLM serving, and this paper argues the tier below DRAM can be cheap S3-compatible object storage rather than expensive remote DRAM pools — with a measured 5.6% TTFT penalty at 64K context that makes the economic case hard to dismiss. It lands on real hardware (100 Gbps RoCE, NIXL, Ceph RGW, DAOS) at exactly the moment serving clusters are being sized around KV cache footprint.
bridge.why_not_higher: It is a prototype-stage systems paper from a storage/DC angle rather than a broadly consumed result, and the headline number depends on long contexts where compute conveniently masks transfer — the 4K case adds 56–75 ms, which is a real regression for short-prompt traffic.
bridge.what_would_raise_priority: Integration into a mainstream serving stack (vLLM/SGLang/LMCache) with end-to-end throughput and cost-per-token numbers at production concurrency, not just TTFT deltas on a prototype cluster.
bridge.one_sentence_episode_hook: Everyone assumes KV cache has to live in fast memory — ObjectCache puts it in S3-compatible object storage and pays only 5.6% more time-to-first-token, by making the storage server hand over the cache layer-by-layer in exactly the order the GPU eats it.

public.title: SWE-Skills-Bench: Do Agent Skills Actually Help in Real-World Software Engineering?
public.abstract: Agent skills, structured procedural knowledge packages injected at inference time, are increasingly used to augment LLM agents on software engineering tasks. However, their real utility in end-to-end development settings remains unclear. We present SWE-Skills-Bench, the first requirement-driven benchmark that isolates the marginal utility of agent skills in real-world software engineering (SWE). It pairs 49 public SWE skills with authentic GitHub repositories pinned at fixed commits and requirement documents with explicit acceptance criteria, yielding approximately 565 task instances across six SWE subdomains. We introduce a deterministic verification framework that maps each task's acceptance criteria to execution-based tests, enabling controlled paired evaluation with and without the skill. Our results show that skill injection benefits are far more limited than rapid adoption suggests: 39 of 49 skills yield zero pass-rate improvement, and the average gain is only +1.2%. Token overhead varies from modest savings to a 451% increase while pass rates remain unchanged. Only seven specialized skills produce meaningful gains (up to +30%), while three degrade performance (up to -10%) due to version-mismatched guidance conflicting with project context. These findings suggest that agent skills are a narrow intervention whose utility depends strongly on domain fit, abstraction level, and contextual compatibility. SWE-Skills-Bench provides a testbed for evaluating the design, selection, and deployment of skills in software engineering agents. SWE-Skills-Bench is available at https://github.com/GeniusHTX/SWE-Skills-Bench.
public.why_now: Agent skills are being adopted across the industry faster than anyone has measured whether they work, and this is the first controlled paired evaluation showing 39 of 49 public skills yield exactly zero pass-rate improvement while some inflate token usage by 451%. A rigorous negative result against a live hype cycle is exactly the kind of thing a broad AI audience wants within weeks, not months.
public.why_not_higher: The memory/storage lens is essentially absent — the token-overhead finding is a context-length cost observation, not a KV cache, offload, or bandwidth story. The evaluation is also confined to software engineering tasks, so the negative result may not generalize to skills in other domains.
downgrade_reasons: no memory/storage mechanism
single-domain evaluation (SWE only)
benchmark paper rather than a new method
public.what_would_raise_priority: Extending the paired methodology beyond SWE to show whether the near-zero marginal utility of skills holds across domains, or connecting the 451% token overhead to concrete serving cost and KV cache behavior.
public.one_sentence_episode_hook: Everyone is shipping agent skills — this benchmark ran 565 controlled A/B tasks against real GitHub repos and found 39 out of 49 skills did literally nothing, some made things worse, and a few quietly quadrupled the token bill for no gain.

public.title: Why AI systems don't learn and what to do about it: Lessons on autonomous learning from cognitive science
public.abstract: We critically examine the limitations of current AI models in achieving autonomous learning and propose a learning architecture inspired by human and animal cognition. The proposed framework integrates learning from observation (System A) and learning from active behavior (System B) while flexibly switching between these learning modes as a function of internally generated meta-control signals (System M). We discuss how this could be built by taking inspiration on how organisms adapt to real-world, dynamic environments across evolutionary and developmental timescales.
public.why_now: Dupoux, LeCun, and Malik jointly arguing that current AI models cannot learn autonomously is a high-visibility position statement from three people whose framing shapes research agendas, and the System A / System B / System M decomposition gives a broad audience a concrete vocabulary for the autonomous-learning debate. Position papers like this drive months of discussion regardless of whether the architecture is ever built.
public.why_not_higher: There are no experiments, no models, and no implementation — it is a proposal, and much of it restates LeCun's long-running world-model and self-supervised-learning arguments with a cognitive-science framing layered on top. Nothing here changes how anyone builds, trains, or serves a system tomorrow.
downgrade_reasons: no empirical results or workloads
memory/storage connection absent
substantial overlap with LeCun's prior position work
first-pass score inflated by influencer and social-momentum boosts rather than content
public.what_would_raise_priority: A concrete instantiation of System M meta-control with measured results on a real learning task, or explicit architectural commitments that implementers could actually test.
public.one_sentence_episode_hook: Three of the most influential names in AI say our models don't actually learn — and their fix borrows from how animals decide when to watch versus when to act.

public.title: Agent Skills for Large Language Models: Architecture, Acquisition, Security, and the Path Forward
public.abstract: The transition from monolithic language models to modular, skill-equipped agents marks a defining shift in how large language models (LLMs) are deployed in practice. Rather than encoding all procedural knowledge within model weights, agent skills -- composable packages of instructions, code, and resources that agents load on demand -- enable dynamic capability extension without retraining. It is formalized in a paradigm of progressive disclosure, portable skill definitions, and integration with the Model Context Protocol (MCP). This survey provides a comprehensive treatment of the agent skills landscape, as it has rapidly evolved during the last few months. We organize the field along four axes: (i) architectural foundations, examining the SKILL$.$md specification, progressive context loading, and the complementary roles of skills and MCP; (ii) skill acquisition, covering reinforcement learning with skill libraries, autonomous skill discovery (SEAgent), and compositional skill synthesis; (iii) deployment at scale, including the computer-use agent (CUA) stack, GUI grounding advances, and benchmark progress on OSWorld and SWE-bench; and (iv) security, where recent empirical analyses reveal that 26.1% of community-contributed skills contain vulnerabilities, motivating our proposed Skill Trust and Lifecycle Governance Framework -- a four-tier, gate-based permission model that maps skill provenance to graduated deployment capabilities. We identify seven open challenges -- from cross-platform skill portability to capability-based permission models -- and propose a research agenda for realizing trustworthy, self-improving skill ecosystems. Unlike prior surveys that broadly cover LLM agents or tool use, this work focuses specifically on the emerging skill abstraction layer and its implications for the next generation of agentic systems. Project repo: https://github.com/scienceaix/agentskills
public.why_now: Agent skills (SKILL.md, progressive disclosure, MCP integration) went from nonexistent to a de-facto deployment pattern in months, and this is the first survey that treats the skill abstraction layer as its own thing rather than a footnote in a tool-use survey. The 26.1%-of-community-skills-are-vulnerable finding lands squarely on a supply-chain question every team shipping agents is about to hit.
public.why_not_higher: It is a survey, so nothing here is independently validated — the security number and the benchmark progress are inherited from other people's work, and the proposed Skill Trust and Lifecycle Governance Framework is a proposal with no deployment evidence behind it. Surveying a field that is three months old also means much of it will be stale fast.
downgrade_reasons: survey, no original experiments
governance framework proposed but unvalidated
fast-moving field, short shelf life
memory/storage connection absent — progressive context loading is prompt-budget management, not KV cache, offload, or tiering
public.what_would_raise_priority: An empirical evaluation of the trust framework against the vulnerable-skill corpus, showing the four-tier gate model actually blocks real attacks without crippling legitimate skills.
public.one_sentence_episode_hook: One in four community-contributed agent skills ships with a vulnerability — and your agent loads them on demand, without retraining, without review: what happens when capability becomes a package manager?

public.title: LLMs Gaming Verifiers: RLVR can Lead to Reward Hacking
public.abstract: As reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has become the dominant paradigm for scaling reasoning capabilities in LLMs, a new failure mode emerges: LLMs gaming verifiers. We study this phenomenon on inductive reasoning tasks, where models must induce and output logical rules. We find that RLVR-trained models systematically abandon rule induction. Instead of learning generalizable patterns (e.g., ``trains carrying red cars go east''), they enumerate instance-level labels, producing outputs that pass verifiers without capturing the relational patterns required by the task. We show that this behavior is not a failure of understanding but a form of reward hacking: imperfect verifiers that check only extensional correctness admit false positives. To detect such shortcuts, we introduce Isomorphic Perturbation Testing (IPT), which evaluates a single model output under both extensional and isomorphic verification, where the latter enforces invariance under logically isomorphic tasks. While genuine rule induction remains invariant, shortcut strategies fail. We find that shortcut behavior is specific to RLVR-trained reasoning models (e.g., GPT-5, Olmo3) and absent in non-RLVR models (e.g., GPT-4o, GPT-4.5, Ministral). Moreover, shortcut prevalence increases with task complexity and inference-time compute. In controlled training experiments, extensional verification directly induces shortcut strategies, while isomorphic verification eliminates them. These results show that RLVR can incentivize reward hacking not only through overt manipulation but also by exploiting what the verifier fails to enforce.
public.why_now: RLVR is the dominant post-training recipe right now, and this paper gives a concrete, mechanistic account of how models game verifiers by exploiting what the verifier fails to check rather than by overt manipulation. The finding that shortcut behavior appears only in RLVR-trained models and grows with inference-time compute is directly relevant to anyone scaling reasoning training.
public.why_not_higher: The evidence lives on inductive rule-induction tasks, a narrow and somewhat synthetic setting, so the leap from 'extensional verifiers admit false positives here' to 'your math or code verifier is being gamed too' is argued rather than demonstrated. The memory/storage lens is absent entirely.
downgrade_reasons: narrow task family (inductive rule induction) as the sole evidence base
no memory or systems connection despite embedding similarity
public.what_would_raise_priority: Reproduction of the shortcut effect and the isomorphic-verification fix on mainstream RLVR domains like math or code, where the verifier gap would have immediate practical consequences.
public.one_sentence_episode_hook: When you pay a model only for correct answers, it learns to memorize the answer key instead of the rule — and this paper shows how to build a verifier that can tell the difference.

public.title: Empty Shelves or Lost Keys? Recall Is the Bottleneck for Parametric Factuality
public.abstract: Standard factuality evaluations of LLMs treat all errors alike, obscuring whether failures arise from missing knowledge (empty shelves) or from limited access to encoded facts (lost keys). We propose a behavioral framework that profiles factual knowledge at the level of facts rather than questions, characterizing each fact by whether it is encoded, and then by how accessible it is: cannot be recalled, can be directly recalled, or can only be recalled with inference-time computation (thinking). To support such profiling, we introduce WikiProfile, a new benchmark constructed via an automated pipeline with a prompted LLM grounded in web search. Across 4 million responses from 13 LLMs, we find that encoding is nearly saturated in frontier models on our benchmark, with GPT-5 and Gemini-3 encoding 95--98% of facts. However, recall remains a major bottleneck: many errors previously attributed to missing knowledge instead stem from failures to access it. These failures are systematic and disproportionately affect long-tail facts and reverse questions. Finally, we show that thinking improves recall and can recover a substantial fraction of failures, indicating that future gains may rely less on scaling and more on methods that improve how models utilize what they already encode.
public.why_now: Hallucination and factuality are the most listener-legible failure modes in LLMs, and this paper reframes them with a clean, testable claim: frontier models already encode 95-98% of the facts they get wrong, so the failure is retrieval from weights, not absence of knowledge. That flips the default assumption that more pretraining scale fixes factuality, and it lands while inference-time compute is the field's central bet.
public.why_not_higher: The encoded-vs-accessible split is a behavioral proxy, not a parametric probe, so 'the fact is in there' rests on the model surfacing it under some prompt condition; a fact recoverable only via thinking is arguably not cleanly distinguishable from one being reconstructed. The benchmark is also LLM-constructed with web search, which risks selecting facts that LLM-shaped pipelines find tractable.
public.what_would_raise_priority: Mechanistic or probing evidence that the 'lost keys' facts are genuinely present in the weights independent of any prompting strategy, plus a method that recovers recall without paying the full thinking-time cost.
public.one_sentence_episode_hook: GPT-5 and Gemini-3 know 95-98% of the facts they get wrong — the shelves aren't empty, the models just can't find the key, and that means the next factuality win comes from better retrieval of what's already inside, not from another order of magnitude of pretraining.

public.title: Soft Contamination Means Benchmarks Test Shallow Generalization
public.abstract: If LLM training data is polluted with benchmark test data, then benchmark performance gives biased estimates of out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization. Typical decontamination filters use n-gram matching which fail to detect semantic duplicates: sentences with equivalent (or near-equivalent) content that are not close in string space. We study this soft contamination of training data by semantic duplicates. Among other experiments, we embed the Olmo3 training corpus and find that: 1) contamination remains widespread, e.g. we find semantic duplicates for 78% of CodeForces and exact duplicates for 50% of ZebraLogic problems; 2) including semantic duplicates of benchmark data in training does improve benchmark performance; and 3) when finetuning on duplicates of benchmark datapoints, performance also improves on truly-held-out datapoints from the same benchmark. We argue that recent benchmark gains are thus confounded: the prevalence of soft contamination means gains reflect both genuine capability improvements and the accumulation of test data and effective test data in growing training corpora.
public.why_now: Every claimed frontier-model benchmark gain rests on the assumption that decontamination worked, and this paper shows n-gram filters miss semantic duplicates at scale — 78% of CodeForces problems have semantic duplicates in the Olmo3 corpus. It also shows finetuning on duplicates lifts scores on truly held-out items from the same benchmark, which means contamination inflates results even where leakage looks clean.
public.why_not_higher: It diagnoses rather than fixes: there is no proposed decontamination method that scales, and the strongest claims rest on one open corpus (Olmo3) since closed-model training data cannot be inspected.
public.what_would_raise_priority: A practical semantic-decontamination pipeline with measured recall against a labeled duplicate set, or replication across a second open corpus such as Dolma or FineWeb.
public.one_sentence_episode_hook: Your decontamination filter is checking for exact copies while the answers walk in wearing a paraphrase — and the benchmark gains everyone is celebrating may be partly the test set coming home.

public.title: When Benchmarks Lie: Evaluating Malicious Prompt Classifiers Under True Distribution Shift
public.abstract: Detecting prompt injection and jailbreak attacks is critical for deploying LLM-based agents safely. As agents increasingly process untrusted data from emails, documents, tool outputs, and external APIs, robust attack detection becomes essential. Yet current evaluation practices and production systems have fundamental limitations. We present a comprehensive analysis using a diverse benchmark of 18 datasets spanning harmful requests, jailbreaks, indirect prompt injections, and extraction attacks. We propose Leave-One-Dataset-Out (LODO) evaluation to measure true out-of-distribution generalization, revealing that the standard practice of train-test splits from the same dataset sources severely overestimates performance: aggregate metrics show an 8.4 percentage point AUC inflation, but per-dataset gaps range from 1% to 25% accuracy-exposing heterogeneous failure modes. To understand why classifiers fail to generalize, we analyze Sparse Auto-Encoder (SAE) feature coefficients across LODO folds, finding that 28% of top features are dataset-dependent shortcuts whose class signal depends on specific dataset compositions rather than semantic content. We systematically compare production guardrails (PromptGuard 2, LlamaGuard) and LLM-as-judge approaches on our benchmark, finding all three fail on indirect attacks targeting agents (7-37% detection) and that PromptGuard 2 and LlamaGuard cannot evaluate agentic tool injection due to architectural limitations. Finally, we show that LODO-stable SAE features provide more reliable explanations for classifier decisions by filtering dataset artifacts. We release our evaluation framework at https://github.com/maxf-zn/prompt-mining to establish LODO as the appropriate protocol for prompt attack detection research.
public.why_now: Prompt injection is the dominant blocker for shipping LLM agents that touch untrusted email, documents, and tool output, and this paper shows the guardrails people actually deploy — PromptGuard 2, LlamaGuard, LLM-as-judge — detect only 7-37% of indirect attacks, with two of them architecturally unable to evaluate agentic tool injection at all. The Leave-One-Dataset-Out result (8.4 AUC points of inflation, per-dataset gaps up to 25%) says the published numbers people trust are measuring dataset memorization, not detection.
public.why_not_higher: Single-author arXiv preprint with no independent replication, and the headline is a negative evaluation result rather than a working defense — it tells you your detector is broken without telling you what to run instead.
public.what_would_raise_priority: Independent reproduction of the LODO gaps by a lab that maintains one of the production guardrails, or a follow-up showing a detector that survives LODO on indirect agentic injection.
public.one_sentence_episode_hook: Your prompt injection detector reports 95% accuracy — then this paper retrains it without one dataset in the mix, and the whole number turns out to be the classifier recognizing which benchmark it's reading rather than recognizing an attack.

public.title: Autorubric: Unifying Rubric-based LLM Evaluation
public.abstract: Techniques for reliable rubric-based LLM evaluation -- ensemble judging, bias mitigation, few-shot calibration -- are scattered across papers with inconsistent terminology and partial implementations. We introduce Autorubric, an open-source framework that unifies these rubric-based LLM evaluation lessons with opinionated defaults: analytic rubrics with binary, ordinal, and nominal criteria; single-judge and ensemble evaluation; few-shot calibration; bias mitigations; and psychometric reliability metrics. We validate on three benchmarks: RiceChem (college chemistry grading, 80\% accuracy with 5-shot calibration), ResearcherBench (deep research evaluation, 931 criteria, cross-judge agreement analysis), and CHARM-100, a new chatbot evaluation dataset combining all three criterion types with ground truth labels (87\% binary accuracy, moderate-to-substantial $κ$). Beyond measurement, per-criterion scores and explanations serve as optimization signals. We demonstrate how Autorubric's rubric-evaluation explanations raise a peer review agent's score from 0.47 to 0.85 (above the 0.82 expert-curated baseline), and its scores serve as RL rewards to produce statistically significant improvement on AdvancedIF (+0.039, Wilcoxon $p = 0.032$) with positive transfer to IFEval. In all of these cases, Autorubric enabled us to rapidly operationalize various rubric design choices and best practices with minimal effort.
public.why_now: LLM-as-judge is now load-bearing infrastructure for nearly every model release, agent eval, and RL reward pipeline, yet the reliability techniques are folklore scattered across papers with no shared implementation. Autorubric consolidates them into an open-source framework with psychometric reliability metrics, and then shows the rubric scores double as optimization signal — a peer-review agent going 0.47 to 0.85, and RL rewards transferring to IFEval.
public.why_not_higher: It is fundamentally a consolidation-and-tooling paper rather than a new idea, and the headline downstream gains are modest (AdvancedIF +0.039) on small benchmarks. Adoption, not novelty, will decide whether it matters.
public.what_would_raise_priority: Evidence that a major lab or open-model release actually used Autorubric rubrics as its RL reward or eval harness, with reliability numbers on frontier-scale runs.
public.one_sentence_episode_hook: Everyone is grading their models with other models — but almost nobody checks whether the grader is any good, and this paper turns that missing rubric into both a measuring stick and a training signal.

public.title: $π_{0.7}$: a Steerable Generalist Robotic Foundation Model with Emergent Capabilities
public.abstract: We present a new robotic foundation model, called $π_{0.7}$, that can enable strong out-of-the-box performance in a wide range of scenarios. $π_{0.7}$ can follow diverse language instructions in unseen environments, including multi-stage tasks with various kitchen appliances, provide zero-shot cross-embodiment generalization, for example enabling a robot to fold laundry without seeing the task before, and perform challenging tasks such as operating an espresso machine out of the box at a level of performance that matches much more specialized RL-finetuned models. The main idea behind $π_{0.7}$ is to use diverse context conditioning during training. This conditioning information, contained in the prompt, makes it possible to steer the model precisely to perform many tasks with different strategies. It is conditioned not just on a language command that describes what it should do, but on additional multimodal information that also describes the manner or strategy in which it should do it, including metadata about task performance and subgoal images. This enables $π_{0.7}$ to use very diverse data, including demonstrations, potentially suboptimal (autonomous) data including failures, and data from non-robot sources. Our experiments evaluate $π_{0.7}$ across numerous tasks with multiple robot platforms, on tasks that require speed and dexterity, language following, and compositional task generalization.
public.why_now: Physical Intelligence's π-series releases draw outsized attention as the clearest public evidence of whether the foundation-model recipe transfers to embodied control, and π₀.₇'s central claim — that conditioning on strategy metadata, subgoal images, and even failure data lets you train on messy heterogeneous corpora — is a data-curation argument that generalizes past robotics. Zero-shot cross-embodiment transfer (a robot folding laundry it never saw) is the kind of concrete result a broad AI audience registers within weeks.
public.why_not_higher: The memory/storage lens is essentially absent — there is no KV cache, offload, bandwidth, or data-movement story here, only incidental multimodal context conditioning. Evaluation is also on proprietary robot fleets with no public reproduction path, so the headline claims can't be independently checked.
downgrade_reasons: no memory, cache, or storage mechanism — robotics control paper
closed model, closed data, closed hardware; results not reproducible
out-of-the-box performance claims rest on in-house task suites rather than shared benchmarks
domain is robotics rather than the serving/training systems the memory lens targets
public.what_would_raise_priority: A released checkpoint, evaluation protocol, or an ablation isolating how much of the gain comes from context conditioning versus data scale would make it coverable now.
public.one_sentence_episode_hook: Physical Intelligence taught a robot to fold laundry it had never seen by feeding it its own failures — the trick is telling the model not just what to do, but how, and it reframes what counts as training data.

public.title: Mobile-Agent-v3.5: Multi-platform Fundamental GUI Agents
public.abstract: The paper introduces GUI-Owl-1.5, the latest native GUI agent model that features instruct/thinking variants in multiple sizes (2B/4B/8B/32B/235B) and supports a range of platforms (desktop, mobile, browser, and more) to enable cloud-edge collaboration and real-time interaction. GUI-Owl-1.5 achieves state-of-the-art results on more than 20+ GUI benchmarks on open-source models: (1) on GUI automation tasks, it obtains 56.5 on OSWorld, 71.6 on AndroidWorld, and 48.4 on WebArena; (2) on grounding tasks, it obtains 80.3 on ScreenSpotPro; (3) on tool-calling tasks, it obtains 47.6 on OSWorld-MCP, and 46.8 on MobileWorld; (4) on memory and knowledge tasks, it obtains 75.5 on GUI-Knowledge Bench. GUI-Owl-1.5 incorporates several key innovations: (1) Hybird Data Flywheel: we construct the data pipeline for UI understanding and trajectory generation based on a combination of simulated environments and cloud-based sandbox environments, in order to improve the efficiency and quality of data collection. (2) Unified Enhancement of Agent Capabilities: we use a unified thought-synthesis pipeline to enhance the model's reasoning capabilities, while placing particular emphasis on improving key agent abilities, including Tool/MCP use, memory and multi-agent adaptation; (3) Multi-platform Environment RL Scaling: We propose a new environment RL algorithm, MRPO, to address the challenges of multi-platform conflicts and the low training efficiency of long-horizon tasks. The GUI-Owl-1.5 models are open-sourced, and an online cloud-sandbox demo is available at https://github.com/X-PLUG/MobileAgent.
public.why_now: GUI agents are one of the few agent categories with a real consumer-facing payoff, and this is an open-weights family spanning 2B to 235B with SOTA open-source numbers on OSWorld, AndroidWorld, and WebArena — people will actually be able to run it. The MRPO environment-RL algorithm for multi-platform training conflicts is the one piece with transferable substance.
public.why_not_higher: This is fundamentally a model-release-plus-benchmark-table paper: the headline is a scoreboard sweep across 20+ benchmarks, and the three 'key innovations' are described at pipeline-diagram altitude rather than in enough detail to reproduce or reason about. It has no memory/storage angle at all.
downgrade_reasons: model release / benchmark sweep rather than a mechanism paper
memory lens signal is keyword contamination — 'memory and knowledge tasks' is agent episodic memory, not KV cache, bandwidth, or storage
innovations (data flywheel, thought synthesis, MRPO) described too thinly to evaluate
cloud-edge collaboration claim is asserted, not measured
public.what_would_raise_priority: A concrete serving-side story — how the 2B/4B edge variants and the 235B cloud model actually split work under latency and KV budget constraints — would give it a real systems spine and a memory-lens path.
public.one_sentence_episode_hook: An open-weights agent that clicks through your desktop, phone, and browser at state-of-the-art rates — and the training trick, MRPO, that stopped those three platforms from fighting each other inside one model.

memory.title: [AAFLOW+] Stateful Operator Abstraction with Zero-Copy Distributed KV Cache Orchestration for Multi-Agent Workflows
memory.abstract: Multi-agent LLM systems increasingly integrate retrieval, planning, and reasoning, but remain fundamentally text-centric, requiring agents to repeatedly recompute shared context through expensive prefill. Although single-request inference is known to be accelerated by KV-cache management, it is usually restricted to local serving scopes. We introduce AAFLOW+, a stateful extension of agentic workflow operators that makes KV cache a first-class distributed systems object. AAFLOW+ builds processes into communication-aware graphs that concurrently optimize data, prompts, and reusable model state. It also provides operators for KV materialization, transfer, fork, composition, and eviction. Its runtime enables zero-copy, transfer-aware execution, allowing agents to reuse long context without recomputation. AAFLOW+ reduces TTFT by up to 50.2x, achieves up to 7.63x reduced multi-agent compute cost at 16-agent scale, reduces KV memory by 1.72-6.10x, and increases throughput by more than 7.74x, based on an analytical cost model parameterized by empirical hardware microbenchmarks. The results demonstrate that KV transmission outperforms recomputation on networks with moderate to high bandwidth, making sure KV-state sharing greatly increases efficiency in multi-agent LLM systems by replacing text passing.
memory.why_now: Multi-agent LLM pipelines burn most of their cost re-prefilling shared context, and this paper treats KV cache as a distributed object with explicit materialize/transfer/fork/compose/evict operators rather than a per-request local artifact. That framing — KV state as the unit of inter-agent communication instead of text — is exactly where multi-agent serving is heading.
memory.why_not_higher: Every headline number (50.2x TTFT, 7.63x cost, 7.74x throughput) comes from an analytical cost model parameterized by microbenchmarks, not a measured end-to-end system, so the speedups are projections rather than results.
downgrade_reasons: results from analytical cost model, not measured end-to-end runs
implausibly large speedup multipliers with no real-workload validation
no accuracy/quality evaluation of forked or composed KV state
16-agent scale claim is modeled, not run
memory.what_would_raise_priority: Real measured end-to-end numbers on a deployed multi-agent workload across actual networked GPUs, with quality parity shown for forked and composed KV state.
memory.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if agents stopped talking to each other in text and started passing raw KV cache instead — and what breaks when the cache, not the prompt, becomes the message?

memory.title: Serving Chain-structured Jobs with Large Memory Footprints with Application to Large Foundation Model Serving
memory.abstract: As a current trend in Artificial Intelligence (AI), large foundation models are increasingly employed as the core of AI services. However, even after training, serving such models at scale remains a challenging task due to their heavy resource footprints, particularly in terms of GPU memory. While recent works revealed unique characteristics of systems serving foundation models that distinguish them from traditional distributed computing systems, there is still a lack of fundamental understanding of the underlying system management problems. This work aims at addressing this gap by extracting a novel problem of "server chain composition" via block placement and cache allocation for serving chainstructured jobs with large memory footprints, which models a fundamental problem in serving large foundation models through pipeline parallelism. After showing the NP-hardness of the optimal solution, the focus is turned to developing scalable algorithms with guaranteed performance under state-of-the-art load balancing. Application of the proposed solution to a distributed large language model (LLM) serving system shows significant reduction of response times compared to state-of-the-art solutions.
memory.why_now: Pipeline-parallel serving of large models is memory-bound in practice, and this paper formalizes block placement plus cache allocation as a single 'server chain composition' problem rather than treating them as separate heuristics. That framing is the useful part: it names a real constraint that vLLM/SGLang-class systems currently solve by hand-tuning.
memory.why_not_higher: It is an NP-hardness result plus approximation algorithms in cs.DC/cs.PF, and the abstract offers no models, hardware, or workload traces behind 'significant reduction of response times' — a broad AI audience will not encounter this within 90 days.
downgrade_reasons: theory-first framing with algorithmic guarantees as the headline contribution
evaluation unspecified: no named model, GPU, or workload in the abstract
cache allocation is memory capacity partitioning, not KV reuse/eviction/offload/tiering
no adoption signal in serving stacks people actually run
memory.what_would_raise_priority: A concrete evaluation on a named open model and GPU fleet showing latency wins over vLLM or SGLang pipeline scheduling, ideally with the algorithm landing in a real serving stack.
memory.one_sentence_episode_hook: Everyone tunes pipeline-parallel LLM serving by feel — this paper proves the underlying placement-and-cache-allocation problem is NP-hard, then shows you can get close anyway.

memory.title: CoCoDiff: Optimizing Collective Communications for Distributed Diffusion Transformer Inference Under Ulysses Sequence Parallelism
memory.abstract: Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) are increasingly adopted in scientific computing, yet growing model sizes and resolutions make distributed multi-GPU inference essential. Ulysses sequence parallelism scales DiT inference but introduces frequent all-to-all collectives that dominate latency. Overlapping these with computation is difficult due to tight data dependencies, large message volumes, and asymmetric interconnect bandwidths.   We introduce CoCoDiff, a distributed DiT inference engine exploiting two observations: (1) V requires only linear projection while Q/K need additional normalization and RoPE, creating opportunities to overlap V's communication with Q/K computation; (2) adjacent denoising steps produce similar tensors, yielding temporal redundancy. CoCoDiff introduces three mechanisms: Tile-Aware Parallel All-to-all (TAPA) decomposes collectives into topology-aligned phases; V-First scheduling hides V's communication behind Q/K computation; and V-Major selective communication transmits only active projections on slow interconnects. On the Aurora supercomputer with four DiT models across 1-8 nodes (up to 96 Intel GPU tiles), CoCoDiff achieves an average speedup of 3.6x, peaking at 8.4x.
memory.why_now: Diffusion Transformers are moving into multi-GPU serving, and Ulysses sequence parallelism makes all-to-all collectives the latency bottleneck — this paper attacks that with dependency-aware scheduling rather than more FLOPs. The V-First insight (V needs only a linear projection while Q/K need RoPE and norm, so V's communication hides behind Q/K compute) is the kind of structural observation that generalizes past this one engine.
memory.why_not_higher: Everything is measured on Aurora's Intel GPU tiles under an Xe Link topology, so the topology-aligned tiling and 'slow interconnect' selective-communication path are tuned to hardware almost no one serving DiTs actually uses. The 3.6x average and 8.4x peak speedups are large enough to imply a weak or unoverlapped baseline rather than a tuned production stack.
downgrade_reasons: single-platform evaluation on Aurora Intel GPUs, no NVLink/InfiniBand results
speedup magnitude suggests an unoptimized baseline
communication scheduling, not memory hierarchy — no KV cache, offload, or capacity story
scientific-computing DiT framing narrows the audience relative to video/image generation serving
memory.what_would_raise_priority: Reproduce TAPA and V-First on NVIDIA multi-node hardware against a tuned overlapped baseline (e.g. xDiT or a production DiT server) and show the gains survive.
memory.one_sentence_episode_hook: Three tensors leave every attention block, but only one of them is ready early — how noticing that V needs no rotary embedding turned a communication-bound diffusion model into a 3.6x faster one on a supercomputer.

memory.title: LEXI: Lossless Exponent Coding for Efficient Inter-Chiplet Communication in Hybrid LLMs
memory.abstract: Data movement overheads increase the inference latency of state-of-the-art large language models (LLMs). These models commonly use the bfloat16 (BF16) format for stable training. Floating-point standards allocate eight bits to the exponent, but our profiling reveals that exponent streams exhibit fewer than 3 bits Shannon entropy, indicating high inherent compressibility. To exploit this potential, we propose LEXI, a novel lossless exponent compression scheme based on Huffman coding. LEXI compresses activations and caches on the fly while storing compressed weights for just-in-time decompression near compute, without sacrificing system throughput and model accuracy. The codecs at the ingress and egress ports of network-on-chip routers sustain the maximum link bandwidth via multi-lane LUT decoders, incurring only 0.09 percent area and energy overheads with GF 22 nm technology. LEXI reduces inter-chiplet communication and end-to-end inference latencies by 33-45 percent and 30-35 percent on modern Jamba, Zamba, and Qwen LLMs implemented on a homogeneous chiplet architecture.
memory.why_now: Chiplet-based accelerators are becoming the default scaling path, and this paper shows the BF16 exponent field carries under 3 bits of real entropy — meaning a third of inter-chiplet traffic on today's LLM inference hardware is provably wasted bits. Lossless compression at the NoC router with 0.09% area cost is a rare case where the free lunch is actually free.
memory.why_not_higher: The evaluation is simulation on a homogeneous chiplet architecture the authors define, not silicon or a shipping accelerator, and the audience that can act on router-level codecs is small. The 30-35% end-to-end latency win depends entirely on interconnect being the bottleneck, which is an architecture-specific assumption.
downgrade_reasons: no silicon or commercial-hardware validation; results rest on the authors' own chiplet model
actionable only for accelerator architects, not for people building or serving models
end-to-end gains assume an interconnect-bound regime that may not hold on other designs
memory.what_would_raise_priority: Silicon or FPGA measurement, or evidence that the exponent-entropy finding holds across model families and quantization formats well enough to justify codecs in HBM/NVLink paths rather than just a research chiplet fabric.
memory.one_sentence_episode_hook: Every BF16 number your model moves carries eight exponent bits, but only three of them mean anything — LEXI puts a Huffman codec in the network router and gets a third of the interconnect back for free.

memory.title: OffloadFS: Leveraging Disaggregated Storage for Computation Offloading
memory.abstract: Disaggregated storage systems improve resource utilization and enable independent scaling of storage and compute resources by separating storage resources from computing resources in data centers. NVMe over fabrics (NVMeoF) is a key technology that underpins the functionality and benefits of disaggregated storage systems. While NVMeoF inherently possesses substantial computing and memory capacity, these resources are often underutilized for tasks beyond simple I/O delegation. This study proposes OffloadFS, a user-level file system that enables offloaded IO-intensive tasks primarily to a disaggregated storage node for near-data processing, with the option to offload to peer compute nodes as well, without the need for distributed lock management. OffloadFS optimizes cache management by reducing interference between threads performing distinct I/O operations. On top of OffloadFS, we develop OffloadDB, which enables RocksDB to offload MemTable flush and compaction operations, and OffloadPrep, which offloads image pre-processing tasks for machine learning to disaggregated storage nodes. Our evaluation shows that OffloadFS improves the performance of RocksDB and machine learning pre-processing tasks by up to 3.36x and 1.85x, respectively, compared to OCFS2.
memory.why_now: Disaggregated storage with NVMeoF is becoming the default datacenter substrate for AI clusters, and this paper argues the storage node's idle compute should absorb IO-intensive work instead of shipping bytes back to the compute tier. The offload framing is directly on the data-movement axis the memory lens cares about.
memory.why_not_higher: The AI connection is incidental — image pre-processing is one of two demo workloads, and nothing here touches model training, serving, KV cache, or GPU-side memory pressure. The headline gains are RocksDB numbers measured against OCFS2, a dated general-purpose baseline rather than a modern disaggregated or near-data filesystem.
downgrade_reasons: AI relevance is a demo workload, not the paper's subject
OCFS2 baseline is weak and dated
no model, GPU, or serving-path evaluation
near-data processing for KV stores is well-trodden ground
memory.what_would_raise_priority: Evidence that storage-node offload measurably relieves a real AI bottleneck — dataloader stalls at training scale, or KV cache tiering and paging on the serving path — benchmarked against a current near-data or disaggregated filesystem.
memory.one_sentence_episode_hook: Your storage array has a CPU sitting idle behind every NVMe drive — what happens when you stop treating it as a dumb disk and start shipping the work to the data instead of the data to the work?

memory.title: OServe: Accelerating LLM Serving via Spatial-Temporal Workload Orchestration
memory.abstract: Serving Large Language Models (LLMs) can benefit immensely from parallelizing both the model and input requests across multiple devices, but incoming workloads exhibit substantial spatial and temporal heterogeneity. Spatially, workloads comprise heterogeneous requests with varying compute and memory demands. Temporally, workload composition varies over time. Nevertheless, existing systems typically assume spatially uniform and temporally stable workloads, employing a homogeneous, static model deployment. This mismatch between the assumption and real-world spatial-temporal heterogeneity results in suboptimal performance. We present OServe, an LLM serving system with heterogeneous and flexible model deployment that addresses both spatial and temporal heterogeneity. First, OServe introduces a novel workload-aware scheduling algorithm that optimizes heterogeneous model deployments according to real-time workload characteristics. Second, OServe proposes an efficient workload-adaptive switching method that migrates model deployments in response to predicted workload changes. Experiments on real-world traces show that OServe improves performance by up to 2$\times$ (average: 1.5$\times$) compared to state-of-the-art serving systems.
memory.why_now: Serving systems are converging on the realization that request mixes are neither uniform nor stationary, and OServe attacks both axes at once by re-planning heterogeneous model deployments against live workload characteristics rather than pinning one static parallelism plan. The 1.5x average on real traces is the kind of result that gets copied into vLLM/SGLang-class schedulers if it holds.
memory.why_not_higher: The memory angle is real but indirect: deployment switching implies moving weights and KV state across devices, yet the abstract never commits to a concrete cache, offload, or bandwidth mechanism, so it lands on the scheduling edge of the memory lens rather than its core. The abstract also names no models, hardware, or trace sources, which makes the 2x ceiling hard to size.
downgrade_reasons: memory/data-movement path is implied by deployment migration, not shown as a cache/offload/bandwidth mechanism
no models, GPU count, or trace provenance disclosed in the abstract
1.5x average is a solid but not category-defining serving win
audience skews to inference-infra practitioners rather than a broad AI audience
memory.what_would_raise_priority: A concrete accounting of what switching costs in bytes and stall time — KV migration volume, weight reload traffic, and how much of the 1.5x survives once that data movement is paid for — would move this onto the memory lens properly and make it coverable now.
memory.one_sentence_episode_hook: Every LLM serving system quietly assumes your traffic looks the same at 3am as at 3pm; OServe throws that assumption out and re-shapes the entire model deployment on the fly — and the interesting question is what that reshuffle costs you in moved bytes.

memory.title: ARGUS: Agentic GPU Optimization Guided by Data-Flow Invariants
memory.abstract: LLM-based coding agents can generate functionally correct GPU kernels, yet their performance remains far below hand-optimized libraries on critical computations such as matrix multiplication, attention, and Mixture-of-Experts (MoE). Peak GPU performance requires coordinated reasoning over tightly coupled optimizations, including tiling, shared-memory staging, software pipelining, and instruction scheduling, while existing agents rely on sparse pass/fail feedback, leaving them unable to diagnose global constraint violations.   We present Argus, an agentic framework that addresses this through data-flow invariants: compile-time specifications encoding how data must be choreographed throughout kernel execution. Argus introduces a tile-based, Pythonic DSL exposing hardware instructions and compiler policies while hiding low-level representations. The DSL provides tag functions to propagate symbolic annotations through data and control flow, and tag assertions to enforce relational constraints at use sites. When violations occur, the compiler returns concrete counterexamples identifying the thread, data element, and program point, enabling dense, structured feedback for targeted fixes. Invariants are verified at compile time via abstract interpretation over a layout algebra and SMT solving, with zero runtime overhead. An in-context reinforcement learning planner learns to select optimizations and synthesize effective invariants, supported by a curated knowledge base of GPU optimization techniques.   We evaluate Argus on the AMD MI300X GPU across GEMM, flash attention, and MoE kernels accounting for over 90% of GPU time in LLM inference. Generated kernels achieve 99-104% of state-of-the-art hand-optimized assembly throughput and are 2-1543x faster than existing agentic systems. Argus further generalizes to 200 KernelBench tasks, solving 100% of Level 1 and 90% of Level 2 problems.
memory.why_now: LLM coding agents writing GPU kernels is a live 2026 thread, and Argus claims parity with hand-tuned assembly (99-104%) on the three kernels that dominate inference time, plus 100%/90% on KernelBench Levels 1/2 — a big jump over prior agentic systems. The AMD MI300X target also matters given the ongoing interest in non-CUDA inference stacks.
memory.why_not_higher: The core contribution is compiler/PL machinery — data-flow invariants, layout algebra, SMT verification — which is a narrower audience than the headline suggests, and the memory angle is about shared-memory staging and tiling for kernel throughput, not KV cache reuse, offload, tiering, or bandwidth reduction at the serving layer.
downgrade_reasons: memory relevance is intra-kernel data choreography, not KV cache/storage/offload systems
single-vendor evaluation (MI300X only); no NVIDIA or cross-architecture generalization shown
2-1543x speedup range over agentic baselines suggests weak baselines rather than a tight comparison
DSL + invariant authoring burden is unclear — how much human specification each kernel needed is not established from the abstract
memory.what_would_raise_priority: Cross-vendor results (MI300X plus H100/B200) with an ablation isolating how much of the win comes from the invariants versus the curated knowledge base, and an accounting of human effort per kernel.
memory.one_sentence_episode_hook: An AI agent just wrote GPU kernels that match hand-tuned assembly — and the trick wasn't a bigger model, it was giving the compiler a way to say exactly which thread broke which rule.

memory.title: Speculative Decoding with a Speculative Vocabulary
memory.abstract: Speculative decoding has rapidly emerged as a leading approach for accelerating language model (LM) inference, as it offers substantial speedups while yielding identical outputs. This relies upon a small draft model, tasked with predicting the outputs of the target model. State-of-the-art speculative decoding methods use a draft model consisting of a single decoder layer and output embedding matrix, with the latter dominating drafting time for the latest LMs. Recent work has sought to address this output distribution bottleneck by reducing the vocabulary of the draft model. Although this can improve throughput, it compromises speculation effectiveness when the target token is out-of-vocabulary. In this paper, we argue for vocabulary speculation as an alternative to a reduced vocabulary. We propose SpecVocab, an efficient and effective method that selects a vocabulary subset per decoding step. Across a variety of tasks, we demonstrate that SpecVocab can achieve a higher acceptance length than state-of-the-art speculative decoding approach, EAGLE-3. Notably, this yields up to an 8.1% increase in average throughput over EAGLE-3.
memory.why_now: Speculative decoding is now the default lossless throughput lever in production LLM serving, and this paper attacks the part everyone hits next: with 128K+ vocabularies, the draft model's output embedding matrix — not its decoder layer — dominates drafting time. Choosing a per-step vocabulary subset instead of a fixed reduced vocabulary is the right framing of that bottleneck.
memory.why_not_higher: The headline result is an 8.1% average throughput gain over EAGLE-3, which is a solid but incremental delta on an already-crowded speculative decoding leaderboard, and the abstract does not pin down hardware, batch regimes, or the cost of the per-step vocabulary selection itself.
downgrade_reasons: single-digit throughput gain over an existing strong baseline
memory angle is LM head weight bandwidth, not KV cache, offload, or storage
no stated hardware or serving regime in the abstract
selection overhead per decoding step unquantified
memory.what_would_raise_priority: Evidence that the gain holds at realistic serving batch sizes and on multiple accelerators, plus a measured breakdown showing the vocabulary selection step does not eat its own bandwidth savings.
memory.one_sentence_episode_hook: Your draft model's real bottleneck isn't thinking — it's reading a 128,000-row output matrix every single token, and SpecVocab argues you should guess which rows you actually need.

memory.title: Tropical: Enhancing SLO Attainment in Disaggregated LLM Serving via SLO-Aware Multiplexing
memory.abstract: To guarantee service quality in transformer based large language model (LLM) serving, it is essential to meet the latency constraints of both the prefill phase (measured by Time-to-First-Token, TTFT) and the decode phase (measured by Time-per-Output-Token, TPOT). Non-disaggregated serving places prefill and decode on the same worker, while disaggregated serving places the prefill and decode on isolated workers. However, no single architecture excels in both TTFT and TPOT metrics. After conducting a root cause analysis, we concluded that indisaggregated LLM serving, prefill execution has minimal interference with decode execution but result in high queuing times. In contrast,non-disaggregated LLM serving effectively reduces queuing times but introduces significant interference between prefills and decodes.
