In order to leverage the best aspects of both non-disaggregated anddisaggregated LLM serving, we have designed and implemented Tropical.Tropical introduces an sevice-level objectives (SLO)-aware multiplexing strategy that balances the queuing time and the interference, enabling the LLM serving to achieve high TTFT and TPOT SLOs simultaneously. Our evaluation of real-world datasets reveals that Tropical outperforms both state-of-the-art non-disaggregated and disaggregated LLM serving systems, achieving up to 2.09 more requests within a 90% SLO attainment. Specially, compared to the disaggregated LLM serving system, Tropicalimproves P90 TTFT performance by 9 with only an 15% reduction in P90 TPOT. Against the non-disaggregated LLM serving systems, Tropicaldelivers a 2.8 performance improvement in P90 TPOT while maintaining the same P90 TTFT.
memory.why_now: Prefill/decode disaggregation is now the default architecture assumption in production LLM serving, and the TTFT-vs-TPOT tradeoff it creates is a live operational pain point for anyone running an inference fleet against SLOs.
memory.why_not_higher: The contribution is a scheduling/multiplexing policy in an already crowded lane (DistServe, Splitwise, Sarathi-Serve chunked prefill all attack the same interference-vs-queuing tension), and the abstract's numbers are garbled — "2.09 more requests", "9" improvement with no unit — with no stated models, hardware, or cluster scale.
downgrade_reasons: crowded lane, incremental over chunked-prefill and existing disaggregation schedulers
malformed/unitless headline numbers in abstract
no disclosed model, GPU, or cluster configuration
memory mechanism is scheduling-side, not KV cache reuse/eviction/offload/tiering
memory.what_would_raise_priority: A clear accounting of the KV cache migration cost between prefill and decode workers — bandwidth, interconnect pressure, and cache residency under multiplexing — plus real hardware and model specs behind the speedup claims.
memory.one_sentence_episode_hook: Split your prefill and decode onto separate machines and your first token gets stuck in line; put them back together and your tokens stutter — Tropical argues you should stop choosing and start multiplexing, and the fight is really about who gets to touch the GPU next.

memory.title: Unlocking Parallelism in Autoregressive Language Models via Speculative Decoding with Progressive Tree Drafting
memory.abstract: Speculative decoding has significantly accelerated Large Language Model (LLM) inference by alleviating memory-bound bottlenecks. However, traditional speculative decoding typically relies on auxiliary draft modules, incurring significant training and communication overhead. Although recent methods attempt to generate drafts within the target model itself, they often fail to fully exploit its latent parallel capacity due to a lack of structural coordination. In this paper, we propose \textbf{Progressive Tree Drafting (PTD)}, which employs a structured, guided parallel drafting strategy to harness the model's parallel potential. By coupling a progressive tree structure with a stepwise pruning mechanism, PTD actively guides the LLM to explore multiple semantic paths in a single forward pass, ensuring both draft diversity and coherence. Experiments demonstrate that PTD achieves up to $2\times$ decoding speedup across various benchmarks while remaining training-free and model-agnostic. Our code is available at: https://github.com/MINE-USTC/PTD.
memory.why_now: Speculative decoding is the standard answer to memory-bandwidth-bound autoregressive decoding, and the self-drafting branch of it (no auxiliary draft model, no training) is where practical deployment interest has moved. PTD is training-free, model-agnostic, and ships code, which lowers the barrier for anyone already serving LLMs.
memory.why_not_higher: The self-speculative decoding space is crowded — Medusa, EAGLE variants, Lookahead, and prior tree-drafting work all occupy this territory — and a 2x ceiling is unremarkable against published EAGLE-class numbers. The abstract names no baselines, no serving stack, no batch sizes, and no hardware, so the speedup claim is unanchored.
downgrade_reasons: near-duplicate of a saturated self-speculative decoding line
unnamed baselines and no hardware/batch context in claims
2x is modest relative to published alternatives
memory link is bandwidth amortization only; no KV cache, offload, or tiering mechanism
memory.what_would_raise_priority: A head-to-head against EAGLE-2/3 and Medusa in a real serving stack (vLLM or SGLang) at production batch sizes, showing where tree drafting's extra KV cache and verification cost stop paying for themselves.
memory.one_sentence_episode_hook: Decoding one token at a time wastes most of your GPU's bandwidth reading weights you'll read again next token — so what happens when you make the model draft its own future in a pruned tree, and where does that trick finally break?

monitor.title: REDSearcher: A Scalable and Cost-Efficient Framework for Long-Horizon Search Agents
monitor.abstract: Large language models are transitioning from generalpurpose knowledge engines to realworld problem solvers, yet optimizing them for deep search tasks remains challenging. The central bottleneck lies in the extreme sparsity of highquality search trajectories and reward signals, arising from the difficulty of scalable longhorizon task construction and the high cost of interactionheavy rollouts involving external tool calls. To address these challenges, we propose REDSearcher, a unified framework that codesigns complex task synthesis, midtraining, and posttraining for scalable searchagent optimization. Specifically, REDSearcher introduces the following improvements: (1) We frame task synthesis as a dualconstrained optimization, where task difficulty is precisely governed by graph topology and evidence dispersion, allowing scalable generation of complex, highquality tasks. (2) We introduce toolaugmented queries to encourage proactive tool use rather than passive recall.(3) During midtraining, we strengthen core atomic capabilities knowledge, planning, and function calling substantially reducing the cost of collecting highquality trajectories for downstream training. (4) We build a local simulated environment that enables rapid, lowcost algorithmic iteration for reinforcement learning experiments. Across both textonly and multimodal searchagent benchmarks, our approach achieves stateoftheart performance. To facilitate future research on longhorizon search agents, we will release 10K highquality complex text search trajectories, 5K multimodal trajectories and 1K text RL query set, and together with code and model checkpoints.
monitor.why_now: Search agents are a live topic and the promised release of 10K text plus 5K multimodal trajectories and a 1K RL query set is the actually useful artifact here, since trajectory scarcity is the real bottleneck for anyone training deep-search agents. The task-synthesis framing (difficulty governed by graph topology and evidence dispersion) is a reusable idea beyond this specific system.
monitor.why_not_higher: This is a single-lab pipeline paper with the usual state-of-the-art-on-our-benchmarks claim and no ablation detail in the abstract to separate which of the four contributions carries the gains; the data release is promised, not delivered.
downgrade_reasons: no memory/storage mechanism — cost reduction is about rollout and tool-call economics, not KV cache, bandwidth, or offload
framework paper with four bundled contributions and no attribution of gains
artifact release is promised, not yet available
local simulated environment for RL raises sim-to-real transfer questions the abstract does not address
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Actually shipping the trajectory sets and checkpoints, plus ablations showing the graph-topology difficulty control transfers to agents trained outside this pipeline.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Everyone wants agents that can search for an hour, but nobody can afford the training data — so this team built a machine that manufactures hard search problems on demand, tuning difficulty by how far apart they scatter the evidence in a knowledge graph.

monitor.title: Look Inward to Explore Outward: Learning Temperature Policy from LLM Internal States via Hierarchical RL
monitor.abstract: Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) trains large language models (LLMs) from sampled trajectories, making decoding strategy a core component of learning rather than a purely inference-time choice. Sampling temperature directly controls the exploration--exploitation trade-off by modulating policy entropy, yet existing methods rely on static values or heuristic adaptations that are decoupled from task-level rewards. We propose Introspective LLM, a hierarchical reinforcement learning framework that learns to control sampling temperature during generation. At each decoding step, the model selects a temperature based on its hidden state and samples the next token from the resulting distribution. Temperature and token policies are jointly optimized from downstream rewards using a coordinate ascent scheme. Experiments on mathematical reasoning benchmarks show that learned temperature policies outperform fixed and heuristic baselines, while exhibiting interpretable exploration behaviors aligned with reasoning uncertainty.
monitor.why_now: Decoding temperature is one of the last hand-tuned knobs in RLVR pipelines, and making it a learned function of hidden state is a clean conceptual move that anyone running RL on reasoning models could try. It sits at the training/inference boundary that a lot of teams are actively tuning right now.
monitor.why_not_higher: Evidence is confined to mathematical reasoning benchmarks with no reported model scale, wall-clock cost, or comparison against strong entropy-control baselines, and the hierarchical RL machinery adds real complexity for gains that may not survive outside math.
downgrade_reasons: no memory/storage mechanism — temperature control never touches KV cache, bandwidth, or data movement
single-domain evaluation (math reasoning only)
model scale and compute overhead unreported
coordinate ascent over two policies is a nontrivial training-stability risk that the abstract does not address
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Results showing the learned temperature policy transfers beyond math — to code, agentic, or general RLVR tasks — at a credible model scale with reported training overhead.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Every RL pipeline for reasoning models picks a sampling temperature by hand and hopes; this paper asks whether the model's own hidden state already knows when it should be guessing versus committing.

monitor.title: KernelBlaster: Continual Cross-Task CUDA Optimization via Memory-Augmented In-Context Reinforcement Learning
monitor.abstract: Optimizing CUDA code across multiple generations of GPU architectures is challenging, as achieving peak performance requires an extensive exploration of an increasingly complex, hardware-specific optimization space. Traditional compilers are constrained by fixed heuristics, whereas finetuning Large Language Models (LLMs) can be expensive. However, agentic workflows for CUDA code optimization have limited ability to aggregate knowledge from prior exploration, leading to biased sampling and suboptimal solutions. We propose KernelBlaster, a Memory-Augmented In-context Reinforcement Learning (MAIC-RL) framework designed to improve CUDA optimization search capabilities of LLM-based GPU coding agents. KernelBlaster enables agents to learn from experience and make systematically informed decisions on future tasks by accumulating knowledge into a retrievable Persistent CUDA Knowledge Base. We propose a novel profile-guided, textual-gradient-based agentic flow for CUDA generation and optimization to achieve high performance across generations of GPU architectures. KernelBlaster guides LLM agents to systematically explore high-potential optimization strategies beyond naive rewrites. Compared to the PyTorch baseline, our method achieves geometric mean speedups of 1.43x, 2.50x, and 1.50x on KernelBench Levels 1, 2, and 3, respectively. We release KernelBlaster as an open-source agentic framework, accompanied by a test harness, verification components, and a reproducible evaluation pipeline.
monitor.why_now: LLM agents writing and tuning CUDA kernels is one of the most closely watched applied-AI-for-systems threads right now, and KernelBlaster adds a persistent, retrievable knowledge base so the agent gets better across tasks and GPU generations instead of resampling blind. Open-source release with a test harness and reproducible evaluation makes it checkable rather than a press-release number.
monitor.why_not_higher: The reported gains are geometric-mean speedups on KernelBench, not end-to-end training or serving wins on a real model, and the headline 'memory-augmented' is agent-side experience retrieval — a text knowledge base — not memory-hierarchy or storage systems work.
downgrade_reasons: memory lens is a keyword collision: 'memory-augmented' means agent experience retrieval, not KV cache/offload/bandwidth
benchmark-only evidence; no end-to-end model-level speedup
KernelBench Level 1 gain (1.43x over PyTorch) is modest and easy to over-read
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: An end-to-end result showing generated kernels surviving in a real training or inference stack, plus a breakdown of which optimizations the knowledge base actually learns (tiling, coalescing, shared-memory reuse) so the memory-hierarchy story becomes concrete rather than nominal.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can an LLM learn to write faster CUDA than the compiler — and does giving it a notebook of everything it tried before actually make it smarter, or just more confident?

monitor.title: STRIDE: Strategic Trajectory Reasoning via Discriminative Estimation for Verifiable Reinforcement Learning
monitor.abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has become an effective post-training paradigm for improving the reasoning abilities of large language models. However, existing RLVR methods typically rely on final-answer correctness to assign trajectory-level rewards, providing sparse supervision and treating all tokens uniformly regardless of their actual contribution to reasoning. Although recent studies introduce intermediate signals such as process rewards, high-entropy tokens, and semantic uncertainty, these signals are often not inherently verifiable and may fail to distinguish beneficial strategic patterns from harmful ones. To address this limitation, we propose STRIDE (Strategic Trajectory Reasoning with Discriminative Estimation), a fine-grained RLVR framework that derives strategic reasoning supervision from verifiable outcomes. STRIDE contrasts successful and failed trajectories within each response group to estimate the outcome-discriminative preference of each $n$-gram strategic pattern, and further combines this signal with reasoning saliency entropy to identify decision-relevant strategic patterns. These patterns are assigned differentiated advantage values during RL optimization, enabling more precise credit assignment while preserving the verifiability of RLVR. Extensive experiments demonstrate that STRIDE consistently improves reasoning performance across diverse models, tasks, and extended settings, including VLMs and agent-based systems.
monitor.why_now: RLVR is the dominant post-training recipe for reasoning models, and sparse trajectory-level rewards are its most-cited weakness; STRIDE proposes a credit-assignment signal derived from verifiable outcomes rather than a learned process reward model, which is the direction the field is actively pushing. Claimed transfer to VLMs and agent systems suggests the method is not tied to one benchmark family.
monitor.why_not_higher: It is one of many 2025-2026 RLVR credit-assignment variants (entropy-token weighting, process rewards, group-contrastive advantages), and the n-gram pattern-matching mechanism looks brittle and hyperparameter-heavy without evidence it survives at frontier scale. The abstract offers no ablation detail, no cost accounting for the per-group discriminative estimation, and no baseline comparison strong enough to separate it from GRPO with tuned advantages.
downgrade_reasons: crowded RLVR credit-assignment subfield; incremental over recent variants
no memory/storage mechanism — RL training method, memory similarity is keyword artifact
abstract-level claims only; 'extensive experiments' unquantified, no scale or cost data
n-gram strategic pattern extraction likely fragile across domains and tokenizers
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Reproducible gains over a well-tuned GRPO baseline at 30B+ scale with released code, plus an ablation isolating the n-gram discriminative signal from the saliency-entropy term.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Every RLVR run tells a model 'you got it right' and nothing about which of the ten thousand tokens actually earned it — STRIDE tries to reverse-engineer credit by asking what the winning trajectories said that the losing ones didn't.

monitor.title: Choosing How to Remember: Adaptive Memory Structures for LLM Agents
monitor.abstract: Memory is critical for enabling large language model (LLM) based agents to maintain coherent behavior over long-horizon interactions. However, existing agent memory systems suffer from two key gaps: they rely on a one-size-fits-all memory structure and do not model memory structure selection as a context-adaptive decision, limiting their ability to handle heterogeneous interaction patterns and resulting in suboptimal performance. We propose a unified framework, FluxMem, that enables adaptive memory organization for LLM agents. Our framework equips agents with multiple complementary memory structures. It explicitly learns to select among these structures based on interaction-level features, using offline supervision derived from downstream response quality and memory utilization. To support robust long-horizon memory evolution, we further introduce a three-level memory hierarchy and a Beta Mixture Model-based probabilistic gate for distribution-aware memory fusion, replacing brittle similarity thresholds. Experiments on two long-horizon benchmarks, PERSONAMEM and LoCoMo, demonstrate that our method achieves average improvements of 9.18% and 6.14%.
monitor.why_now: Agent memory is a live design question as long-horizon assistants ship, and framing memory-structure choice as a learned, context-adaptive decision rather than a fixed schema is a reasonable contribution to that conversation. LoCoMo and PERSONAMEM are the benchmarks people currently argue about.
monitor.why_not_higher: The word 'memory' here means agent context organization, not memory/storage systems — there is no KV cache, offload, bandwidth, or tiering behavior anywhere in it, so the high sim_memory is keyword bleed. Gains of 9.18% and 6.14% on two benchmarks are incremental and there is no evidence the learned selector transfers past the interaction distributions it was supervised on.
downgrade_reasons: memory lens is semantic/agent memory, not storage or cache
no systems path to KV reuse, eviction, offload, or bandwidth
modest gains on two benchmarks only
selector trained on offline supervision, transfer unproven
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Evidence that the three-level hierarchy and Beta Mixture gate actually reduce context tokens, retrieval cost, or KV footprint at serving time, rather than only moving benchmark accuracy.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Every agent memory system picks one structure and hopes — this one learns which structure to use per conversation, and the interesting part is that a probabilistic gate beats the similarity threshold everyone else hand-tunes.

monitor.title: SETA: Scaling Environments for Terminal Agents
monitor.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are rapidly shifting toward agents that solve tasks through diverse interfaces, including web and graphical user interfaces (GUIs). Among these, the terminal command line provides a text-based, general-purpose interface, covering tasks from system operations to data science and machine learning. However, scaling terminal-agent training remains challenging, as it requires diverse and coherent task instructions, executable environments, and reliable verification, while lacking naturally grounded supervision data. In this work, we propose SETA, a scalable framework for generating verifiable terminal environments for reinforcement learning (RL). The framework consists of two pipelines sharing a unified verification mechanism: SETA-Synth converts diverse sources into standardized RL environments, and SETA-Evol further expands from existing environments with adaptive control of difficulty and diversity. Together, we construct and release SETA-Env, the largest open-source verifiable terminal RL dataset to date, containing over 4,500 environments. We evaluate our dataset by training Qwen3-8B with GRPO on SETA-Env, achieving 12% pass rate on Terminal-Bench 2.0, the best reported result for an RL-trained model at the 8B scale. We further observe gains on DeepSeek-V4-Flash under the same terminal agent harness, with pass@1 on Terminal-Bench 2.0 improving from 40% to 43% and pass@5 improving from 54% to 58%. These results demonstrate that SETA- Env provides high-quality training environments for terminal agents and serves as a valuable resource for advancing research on terminal-based agent learning.
monitor.why_now: Terminal agents are a live commercial front (coding agents, ops automation), and SETA releases the largest open verifiable terminal RL environment set at 4,500+ environments with a reproducible GRPO recipe. Open environment data is the current bottleneck for agent RL, so the artifact matters more than the numbers.
monitor.why_not_higher: The headline result is a 12% pass rate at 8B and a 3-point bump on a frontier model — real but modest, and the contribution is a dataset plus generation pipeline rather than a method that changes how anyone builds or serves models. Memory/storage relevance is absent; RL environment scaling is not a data-movement story.
downgrade_reasons: no memory/storage mechanism — RL environment generation, not cache or bandwidth behavior
dataset/benchmark contribution rather than a transferable method
modest absolute results (12% at 8B, +3 points on DeepSeek-V4-Flash)
verification quality of synthesized environments unaudited in the abstract
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Evidence that SETA-Env transfers beyond Terminal-Bench — for example gains on unrelated agent harnesses, or an analysis showing which environment properties actually drive the improvement.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Everyone wants agents that can drive a shell, but nobody has enough shells to train them in — SETA manufactures 4,500 verifiable terminal worlds, and the results tell us how far synthetic environments can carry an agent.

monitor.title: Synergistic Intra- and Cross-Layer Regularization Losses for MoE Expert Specialization
monitor.abstract: Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models scale Transformers efficiently but suffer from expert overlap -- redundant representations across experts and routing ambiguity, resulting in severely underutilized model capacity. While architectural solutions like DeepSeekMoE promote specialization, they require substantial structural modifications and rely solely on intra-layer signals. In this paper, we propose two plug-and-play regularization losses that enhance MoE specialization and routing efficiency without modifying router or model architectures. First, an intra-layer specialization loss penalizes cosine similarity between experts' SwiGLU activations on identical tokens, encouraging experts to specialize in complementary knowledge. Second, a cross-layer coupling loss maximizes joint Top-$k$ routing probabilities across adjacent layers, establishing coherent expert pathways through network depth while reinforcing intra-layer expert specialization. Both losses are orthogonal to the standard load-balancing loss and compatible with both the shared-expert architecture in DeepSeekMoE and vanilla top-$k$ MoE architectures. We implement both losses as a drop-in Megatron-LM module. Extensive experiments across pre-training, fine-tuning, and zero-shot benchmarks demonstrate consistent task gains, higher expert specialization, and lower-entropy routing; together, these improvements translate into faster inference via more stable expert pathways.
monitor.why_now: MoE is the dominant scaling architecture for frontier open-weight models, and expert overlap is a real, well-documented capacity leak; two drop-in Megatron-LM losses that improve specialization without touching the router or architecture are cheap enough that labs could actually try them. The plug-and-play framing is the interesting part, not the losses themselves.
monitor.why_not_higher: The memory/storage connection is incidental — lower-entropy routing and 'more stable expert pathways' are asserted to help inference, but the paper offers no concrete path to expert caching, offload, prefetch, or bandwidth reduction. Evidence is also the standard pre-train/fine-tune/zero-shot benchmark sweep with no stated model scale, so it is unclear whether the gains survive at the scale where MoE actually matters.
downgrade_reasons: memory relevance is incidental, not mechanistic — no cache, offload, or bandwidth path
no disclosed model scale or compute budget; unclear if gains hold beyond small MoE
inference speedup claimed as a downstream side effect, not measured as a systems result
auxiliary-loss tuning papers have a poor track record of surviving contact with real pre-training runs
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Measurement at a real scale (10B+ total params) showing specialization gains that translate into a concrete serving win — expert-cache hit rate, reduced expert-weight traffic, or measured throughput under offload.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Mixture-of-Experts models quietly waste a chunk of their capacity because their experts keep learning the same thing — this paper claims you can fix it with two loss terms and zero architecture changes, which raises the harder question of why we bolted on shared experts in the first place.

monitor.title: AdaMem: Adaptive User-Centric Memory for Long-Horizon Dialogue Agents
monitor.abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents increasingly rely on external memory to support long-horizon interaction, personalized assistance, and multi-step reasoning. However, existing memory systems still face three core challenges: they often rely too heavily on semantic similarity, which can miss evidence crucial for user-centric understanding; they frequently store related experiences as isolated fragments, weakening temporal and causal coherence; and they typically use static memory granularities that do not adapt well to the requirements of different questions. We propose AdaMem, an adaptive user-centric memory framework for long-horizon dialogue agents. AdaMem organizes dialogue history into working, episodic, persona, and graph memories, enabling the system to preserve recent context, structured long-term experiences, stable user traits, and relation-aware connections within a unified framework. At inference time, AdaMem first resolves the target participant, then builds a question-conditioned retrieval route that combines semantic retrieval with relation-aware graph expansion only when needed, and finally produces the answer through a role-specialized pipeline for evidence synthesis and response generation. We evaluate AdaMem on the LoCoMo and PERSONAMEM benchmarks for long-horizon reasoning and user modeling. Experimental results show that AdaMem achieves state-of-the-art performance on both benchmarks. The code will be released upon acceptance.
monitor.why_now: Agent memory for long-horizon dialogue is an active area with real product pull, and question-conditioned retrieval routing that only escalates to graph expansion when needed is a sensible efficiency idea. But this is a framework-composition paper validated on two conversational benchmarks, not a result that changes how systems are built.
monitor.why_not_higher: The word 'memory' here means cognitive/agentic memory — working, episodic, persona, graph — not memory/storage systems. There is no KV cache, eviction, offload, tiering, paging, or bandwidth story anywhere in it, so it scores near zero on the memory lens despite heavy keyword overlap. Evidence is LoCoMo and PERSONAMEM SOTA with no ablation detail visible in the abstract and code only promised on acceptance.
downgrade_reasons: agentic memory, not memory/storage systems
keyword overlap on 'memory' inflates first-pass memory score
benchmark-only evidence on two conversational datasets
four-component framework: unclear which part carries the gains
code not released
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Ablations isolating which memory type actually drives the gains, plus retrieval latency and token-cost numbers showing the adaptive route is cheaper than always-on graph expansion.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Every agent framework now ships a 'memory system' — but when a paper stacks working, episodic, persona, and graph memory and reports state of the art, which layer is doing the work, and would three of them do just as well?

monitor.title: ProbeLLM: Automating Principled Diagnosis of LLM Failures
monitor.abstract: Understanding how and why large language models (LLMs) fail is becoming a central challenge as models rapidly evolve and static evaluations fall behind. While automated probing has been enabled by dynamic test generation, existing approaches often discover isolated failure cases, lack principled control over exploration, and provide limited insight into the underlying structure of model weaknesses. We propose ProbeLLM, a benchmark-agnostic automated probing framework that elevates weakness discovery from individual failures to structured failure modes. ProbeLLM formulates probing as a hierarchical Monte Carlo Tree Search, explicitly allocating limited probing budgets between global exploration of new failure regions and local refinement of recurring error patterns. By restricting probing to verifiable test cases and leveraging tool-augmented generation and verification, ProbeLLM grounds failure discovery in reliable evidence. Discovered failures are further consolidated into interpretable failure modes via failure-aware embeddings and boundary-aware induction. Across diverse benchmarks and LLMs, ProbeLLM reveals substantially broader, cleaner, and more fine-grained failure landscapes than static benchmarks and prior automated methods, supporting a shift from case-centric evaluation toward principled weakness discovery.
monitor.why_now: Static benchmarks are visibly saturating and contaminated, so automated, budget-aware probing that clusters individual failures into named failure modes is a live concern for anyone shipping or evaluating models. The MCTS framing with an explicit explore/refine budget split is a cleaner story than the usual grab-bag of adversarial test generators.
monitor.why_not_higher: It is an evaluation-methodology paper whose payoff depends entirely on whether the induced failure modes are real structure or artifacts of the embedding and induction steps, and the abstract offers comparative adjectives ("broader, cleaner, more fine-grained") rather than a metric anyone can check. Nothing here touches serving, training, or memory behavior.
downgrade_reasons: no memory/storage mechanism, direct or adjacent
claims stated qualitatively; no reported numbers or human validation of induced failure modes in the abstract
verifiable-test-case restriction narrows coverage to checkable domains, unclear how much of real LLM failure that captures
crowded space — automated red-teaming and dynamic eval papers arrive weekly
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Evidence that the discovered failure modes hold up under human audit and predict downstream failures on held-out tasks, plus a released tool people actually run against frontier models.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Every benchmark you trust is a snapshot of failures someone already thought to look for — this paper asks whether a search algorithm can go find the failures nobody named yet, and turn a pile of broken cases into a map of how the model is actually broken.

monitor.title: Knowledge Localization in Mixture-of-Experts LLMs Using Cross-Lingual Inconsistency
monitor.abstract: Modern LLMs continue to exhibit significant variance in behavior across languages, such as being able to recall factual information in some languages but not others. While typically studied as a problem to be mitigated, in this work, we propose leveraging this cross-lingual inconsistency as a tool for interpretability in mixture-of-experts (MoE) LLMs. Our knowledge localization framework contrasts routing for sets of languages where the model correctly recalls information from languages where it fails. This allows us to isolate model components that play a functional role in answering about a piece of knowledge. Our method proceeds in two stages: (1) querying the model with difficult factual questions across a diverse set of languages to generate "success" and "failure" activation buckets and then (2) applying a statistical contrastive analysis to the MoE router logits to identify experts important for knowledge. To validate the necessity of this small number of experts for answering a knowledge question, we deactivate them and re-ask the question. We find that despite only deactivating about 20 out of 6000 experts, the model no longer answers correctly in over 40% of cases. Generally, this method provides a realistic and scalable knowledge localization approach to address increasingly complex LLMs.
monitor.why_now: MoE is the dominant frontier architecture, and this turns a known failure mode — models that know a fact in English but not Swahili — into an interpretability probe that pins knowledge to ~20 of 6000 experts. The causal ablation check (deactivate those experts, correctness drops in over 40% of cases) makes it more than a correlational attribution story.
monitor.why_not_higher: It is a diagnostic method with no demonstrated downstream use for editing, routing, serving, or safety, and the memory lens is essentially absent — expert routing here is an interpretability signal, not a path to cache, offload, or bandwidth behavior.
downgrade_reasons: no memory/storage or systems mechanism
interpretability probe with no demonstrated downstream application
validation appears limited to factual recall QA and likely a narrow set of MoE models
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Show the localized experts drive something concrete — targeted knowledge editing, unlearning, cross-lingual repair, or expert prefetch/offload decisions in a real serving stack.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: A model that knows a fact in English but blanks in Swahili is leaking its own wiring diagram — and following that leak lets you switch off 20 experts out of 6000 and make the model forget.

monitor.title: AgentSPEX: An Agent SPecification and EXecution Language
monitor.abstract: Language-model agent systems commonly rely on reactive prompting, in which a single instruction guides the model through an open-ended sequence of reasoning and tool-use steps, leaving control flow and intermediate state implicit and making agent behavior potentially difficult to control. Orchestration frameworks such as LangGraph, DSPy, and CrewAI impose greater structure through explicit workflow definitions, but tightly couple workflow logic with Python, making agents difficult to maintain and modify. In this paper, we introduce AgentSPEX, an Agent SPecification and EXecution Language for specifying LLM-agent workflows with explicit control flow and modular structure, along with a customizable agent harness. AgentSPEX supports typed steps, branching and loops, parallel execution, reusable submodules, and explicit state management, and these workflows execute within an agent harness that provides tool access, a sandboxed virtual environment, and support for checkpointing, verification, and logging. Furthermore, we provide a visual editor with synchronized graph and workflow views for authoring and inspection. We include ready-to-use agents for deep research and scientific research, and we evaluate AgentSPEX on 7 benchmarks. Finally, we show through a user study that AgentSPEX provides a more interpretable and accessible workflow-authoring paradigm than a popular existing agent framework.
monitor.why_now: Agent orchestration is where a lot of practitioner pain currently lives, and a declarative spec language that decouples workflow logic from Python is a direct response to the LangGraph/DSPy/CrewAI maintenance problem people complain about daily. The visual editor plus checkpointing/verification harness makes it more than a paper artifact.
monitor.why_not_higher: This is a framework-and-DSL paper competing in a crowded field where adoption, not benchmarks, decides relevance, and the evidence rests on self-selected benchmarks plus a small user study rather than evidence anyone outside the authors builds with it. The memory/storage lens is essentially absent — control flow and state management here mean workflow variables, not KV cache, offload, or tiering.
downgrade_reasons: no memory/storage mechanism; explicit state management is workflow-level, not cache/storage-level
framework paper whose value depends on adoption not yet demonstrated
user study and benchmark suite are author-selected and likely small-N
crowded space; incremental over existing orchestration frameworks
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Real adoption signal outside the authors — third-party agents shipped in AgentSPEX, or evidence the DSL meaningfully cuts failure rates on long-horizon agent tasks versus reactive prompting.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Every agent framework promises structure and delivers a pile of Python — AgentSPEX asks whether agent workflows deserve their own language, and what you gain when control flow stops being implicit.

monitor.title: MM-Doc-R1: Training Agents for Long Document Visual Question Answering through Multi-turn Reinforcement Learning
monitor.abstract: Conventional Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems often struggle with complex multi-hop queries over long documents due to their single-pass retrieval. We introduce MM-Doc-R1, a novel framework that employs an agentic, vision-aware workflow to address long document visual question answering through iterative information discovery and synthesis. To incentivize the information seeking capabilities of our agents, we propose Similarity-based Policy Optimization (SPO), addressing baseline estimation bias in existing multi-turn reinforcement learning (RL) algorithms like GRPO. Our core insight is that in multi-turn RL, the more semantically similar two trajectories are, the more accurate their shared baseline estimation becomes. Leveraging this, SPO calculates a more precise baseline by similarity-weighted averaging of rewards across multiple trajectories, unlike GRPO which inappropriately applies the initial state's baseline to all intermediate states. This provides a more stable and accurate learning signal for our agents, leading to superior training performance that surpasses GRPO. Our experiments on the MMLongbench-Doc benchmark show that MM-Doc-R1 outperforms previous baselines by 10.4%. Furthermore, SPO demonstrates superior performance over GRPO, boosting results by 5.0% with Qwen3-8B and 6.1% with Qwen3-4B. These results highlight the effectiveness of our integrated framework and novel training algorithm in advancing the state-of-the-art for complex, long-document visual question answering.
monitor.why_now: Multi-turn RL credit assignment is a live problem, and SPO's claim that GRPO misapplies the initial-state baseline to intermediate states is a real, generalizable critique of a widely used algorithm. That critique, not the document-VQA wrapper, is what a broad audience would care about.
monitor.why_not_higher: The evidence is confined to one benchmark (MMLongbench-Doc) at 4B-8B scale, so SPO's generality beyond vision-aware document agents is asserted rather than demonstrated. The memory/storage lens connection is absent — iterative retrieval over long documents is a retrieval-policy question, not a KV cache, offload, or bandwidth question.
downgrade_reasons: no memory/storage mechanism; 'long document' and 'retrieval' are semantic, not systems, notions
single benchmark, single model family, small scale
general algorithmic claim (SPO) evaluated only inside a narrow agentic DocVQA setting
similarity-weighted baseline lacks ablation isolating it from the agentic workflow gains
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: SPO evaluated as a standalone drop-in replacement for GRPO across unrelated multi-turn RL tasks and larger models, with the baseline-estimation improvement ablated apart from the agentic workflow.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: GRPO judges every move in a long agent trajectory against the score it expected before the agent took a single step — MM-Doc-R1's authors argue that's the bug, and that trajectories which look alike should grade each other.

monitor.title: On-Policy Context Distillation for Language Models
monitor.abstract: Context distillation enables language models to internalize in-context knowledge into their parameters. In our work, we propose On-Policy Context Distillation (OPCD), a framework that bridges on-policy distillation with context distillation by training a student model on its own generated trajectories while minimizing reverse Kullback-Leibler divergence against a context-conditioned teacher. We demonstrate the effectiveness of OPCD on two important applications: experiential knowledge distillation, where models extract and consolidate transferable knowledge from their historical solution traces, and system prompt distillation, where models internalize beneficial behaviors encoded in optimized prompts. Across mathematical reasoning, text-based games, and domain-specific tasks, OPCD consistently outperforms baseline methods, achieving higher task accuracy while better preserving out-of-distribution capabilities. We further show that OPCD enables effective cross-size distillation, where smaller student models can internalize experiential knowledge from larger teachers.
monitor.why_now: Context distillation is the natural counterweight to the industry's habit of stuffing ever-longer system prompts and accumulated experience into the context window; OPCD offers a principled on-policy recipe (train on student trajectories, reverse-KL against a context-conditioned teacher) for moving that knowledge into weights instead. The cross-size distillation result — small students internalizing experiential knowledge from larger teachers — is the kind of finding that shapes how teams build agent memory.
monitor.why_not_higher: It is an on-policy distillation training method, not a systems paper, and the results are on mathematical reasoning, text games, and domain tasks rather than a deployed serving stack. The technique also sits close to a crowded space of on-policy distillation and prompt-internalization work, so its distinctiveness is more in the combination than in any single mechanism.
downgrade_reasons: memory/storage connection is indirect: prompt-to-weights internalization implies shorter contexts and smaller KV footprint, but the paper offers no cache, offload, bandwidth, or serving measurement
evaluated on reasoning benchmarks and games rather than realistic production workloads or hardware
adjacent to existing on-policy distillation literature; incremental rather than a new capability
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Concrete serving-side numbers showing that internalizing a long system prompt or experience buffer cuts prefill cost, KV cache residency, or context length at fixed quality would turn this from a training-recipe paper into a genuine bridge paper.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Every long system prompt you ship is rent you pay on every single request — what if the model could just learn it once and stop re-reading it?

monitor.title: Online Experiential Learning for Language Models
monitor.abstract: The prevailing paradigm for improving large language models relies on offline training with human annotations or simulated environments, leaving the rich experience accumulated during real-world deployment entirely unexploited. We propose Online Experiential Learning (OEL), a framework that enables language models to continuously improve from their own deployment experience. OEL operates in two stages: first, transferable experiential knowledge is extracted and accumulated from interaction trajectories collected on the user side; second, this knowledge is consolidated into model parameters via on-policy context distillation, requiring no access to the user-side environment. The two stages are iterated to form an online learning loop, where the improved model collects higher-quality trajectories that yield richer experiential knowledge for subsequent rounds. We evaluate OEL on text-based game environments across multiple model scales and both thinking and non-thinking variants. OEL achieves consistent improvements over successive iterations, enhancing both task accuracy and token efficiency while preserving out-of-distribution performance. Our analysis further shows that extracted experiential knowledge is significantly more effective than raw trajectories, and that on-policy consistency between the knowledge source and the policy model is critical for effective learning.
monitor.why_now: Continual learning from deployment traffic is a live question as labs look past static offline training, and OEL's split between extracting transferable experiential knowledge and consolidating it via on-policy context distillation is a clean framing of that loop. The finding that distilled knowledge beats raw trajectories, and that on-policy consistency between knowledge source and policy is load-bearing, is the part with legs.
monitor.why_not_higher: The evaluation lives entirely in text-based game environments, which is a long way from the real deployment traffic the framing promises, and there is no privacy, infrastructure, or serving-side accounting for what "collected on the user side" actually costs. Without evidence on real user interactions the central claim stays aspirational.
downgrade_reasons: evaluated only on text-game environments, not real deployment traffic
no memory/storage mechanism — trajectory accumulation is incidental, not a cache/offload/bandwidth path
on-policy context distillation is a training-loop contribution with no serving-systems detail
user-side data collection raises privacy/infrastructure questions the paper does not address
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Results from an actual deployed system with real user trajectories, plus accounting for the cost and privacy handling of the collection and distillation loop.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Every model in production is accumulating experience it immediately throws away — this paper asks what it would take to close the loop and let deployment itself become the training signal.

monitor.title: Me, Myself, and $π$ : Evaluating and Explaining LLM Introspection
monitor.abstract: A hallmark of human intelligence is Introspection-the ability to assess and reason about one's own cognitive processes. Introspection has emerged as a promising but contested capability in large language models (LLMs). However, current evaluations often fail to distinguish genuine meta-cognition from the mere application of general world knowledge or text-based self-simulation. In this work, we propose a principled taxonomy that formalizes introspection as the latent computation of specific operators over a model's policy and parameters. To isolate the components of generalized introspection, we present Introspect-Bench, a multifaceted evaluation suite designed for rigorous capability testing. Our results show that frontier models exhibit privileged access to their own policies, outperforming peer models in predicting their own behavior. Furthermore, we provide causal, mechanistic evidence explaining both how LLMs learn to introspect without explicit training, and how the mechanism of introspection emerges via attention diffusion.
monitor.why_now: Introspection in LLMs is an actively contested public debate, and this paper tries to do what most introspection claims skip: separate genuine privileged access to one's own policy from world-knowledge guessing or self-simulation. The claim that frontier models predict their own behavior better than peer models predict it is the kind of falsifiable result the field needs.
monitor.why_not_higher: It is fundamentally a benchmark-plus-interpretability paper with no path to changing how anyone builds, trains, or serves systems, and the 'causal, mechanistic evidence' for attention diffusion is asserted in the abstract without any indication of scale, model coverage, or how the counterfactuals were constructed.
downgrade_reasons: no memory/storage mechanism — attention and parameter references are incidental, not systems-relevant
benchmark result rather than a method that transfers
mechanistic claims unverified from abstract alone; likely small or open-weight model subset
self-prediction advantage has known confounds (shared decoding style, self-recognition) that need explicit controls
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Evidence that the privileged-access result holds across several frontier model families with controls ruling out self-recognition and stylistic self-similarity, plus a concrete demonstration that the attention-diffusion mechanism predicts or steers introspective accuracy.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: When a model tells you what it will do next, is it reading its own mind or just doing a good impression of itself — and does anyone have a test that can tell the difference?

monitor.title: ConfLayers: Adaptive Confidence-based Layer Skipping for Self-Speculative Decoding
monitor.abstract: Self-speculative decoding is an inference technique for large language models designed to speed up generation without sacrificing output quality. It combines fast, approximate decoding using a compact version of the model as a draft model with selective re-evaluation by the full target model. Some existing methods form the draft model by dynamically learning which layers to skip during inference, effectively creating a smaller subnetwork to speed up computation. However, using heuristic-based approaches to select layers to skip can often be simpler and more effective. In this paper, we propose ConfLayers, a dynamic plug-and-play approach to forming the draft model in self-speculative decoding via confidence-based intermediate layer skipping. The process iteratively computes confidence scores for all layers, selects layers to skip based on an adaptive threshold, evaluates the performance of the resulting set, and updates the best selection until no further improvement is achieved or a maximum number of iterations is reached. This framework avoids the overhead and complexity of training a layer skipping policy and can provide more consistent speed-quality trade-offs while preserving the adaptivity of the draft model to diverse tasks and datasets. The performance evaluation of ConfLayers across different models and datasets shows that our novel approach offers up to 1.4x speedup over vanilla LLM generation.
monitor.why_now: Self-speculative decoding is an active serving-efficiency thread, and the claim that a training-free confidence heuristic beats learned layer-skip policies (LayerSkip, SWIFT, Draft&Verify) is the kind of simple-beats-complex result practitioners act on immediately. Plug-and-play with no policy training lowers the adoption bar for anyone already running speculative decoding.
monitor.why_not_higher: 1.4x over vanilla generation is modest for this literature, and the abstract gives no baseline comparison against the learned-policy methods it claims to beat, no model scale, and no serving hardware. The mechanism is compute skipping, not memory or cache behavior — the KV sharing between draft and target is inherited from self-speculative decoding generally, not a contribution here.
downgrade_reasons: speedup ceiling (1.4x) modest for the speculative decoding literature
no head-to-head numbers against learned layer-skip baselines in the abstract
iterative search over layer subsets has unstated calibration cost
memory relevance is incidental to self-speculative decoding, not a contribution
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Head-to-head wins over LayerSkip/SWIFT at 30B+ scale with reported acceptance rates and batched-serving throughput, or evidence that layer skipping meaningfully cuts KV cache footprint or bandwidth rather than just FLOPs.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Everyone is training neural policies to decide which transformer layers a draft model can skip — this paper says just ask the layers how confident they are, and get the same speedup for free.

monitor.title: SafeNeuron: Neuron-Level Safety Alignment for Large Language Models
monitor.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) and multimodal LLMs are typically safety-aligned before release to prevent harmful content generation. However, recent studies show that safety behaviors are concentrated in a small subset of parameters, making alignment brittle and easily bypassed through neuron-level attacks. Moreover, most existing alignment methods operate at the behavioral level, offering limited control over the model's internal safety mechanisms. In this work, we propose SafeNeuron, a neuron-level safety alignment framework that improves robustness by redistributing safety representations across the network. SafeNeuron first identifies safety-related neurons, then freezes these neurons during preference optimization to prevent reliance on sparse safety pathways and force the model to construct redundant safety representations. Extensive experiments across models and modalities demonstrate that SafeNeuron significantly improves robustness against neuron pruning attacks, reduces the risk of open-source models being repurposed as red-team generators, and preserves general capabilities. Furthermore, our layer-wise analysis reveals that safety behaviors are governed by stable and shared internal representations. Overall, SafeNeuron provides an interpretable and robust perspective for model alignment.
monitor.why_now: Safety alignment in open-weight models is known to be brittle because it concentrates in a sparse set of parameters, and this paper attacks that directly by freezing safety neurons during preference optimization to force redundant safety pathways. That framing is timely as open-weight releases keep getting repurposed as red-team generators.
monitor.why_not_higher: There is no memory/storage connection at all, and the abstract reports no concrete numbers, baselines, or model scales, so the robustness claim is unverifiable from the text alone. Neuron-level interpretability plus alignment is also a crowded space with many similar-sounding methods.
downgrade_reasons: no memory/storage mechanism, direct or adjacent
no quantitative results or named baselines in the abstract
crowded neuron-attribution + alignment area
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Concrete numbers showing pruning-attack robustness holds at real open-weight scales (Llama/Qwen class) with quantified capability retention against strong alignment baselines.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Safety alignment lives in a shockingly small handful of neurons — so what happens if you freeze exactly those neurons and force the model to learn to refuse in a dozen redundant ways instead?

monitor.title: Reward Hacking in the Era of Large Models: Mechanisms, Emergent Misalignment, Challenges
monitor.abstract: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) and related alignment paradigms have become central to steering large language models (LLMs) and multimodal large language models (MLLMs) toward human-preferred behaviors. However, these approaches introduce a systemic vulnerability: reward hacking, where models exploit imperfections in learned reward signals to maximize proxy objectives without fulfilling true task intent. As models scale and optimization intensifies, such exploitation manifests as verbosity bias, sycophancy, hallucinated justification, benchmark overfitting, and, in multimodal settings, perception--reasoning decoupling and evaluator manipulation. Recent evidence further suggests that seemingly benign shortcut behaviors can generalize into broader forms of misalignment, including deception and strategic gaming of oversight mechanisms. In this survey, we propose the Proxy Compression Hypothesis (PCH) as a unifying framework for understanding reward hacking. We formalize reward hacking as an emergent consequence of optimizing expressive policies against compressed reward representations of high-dimensional human objectives. Under this view, reward hacking arises from the interaction of objective compression, optimization amplification, and evaluator--policy co-adaptation. This perspective unifies empirical phenomena across RLHF, RLAIF, and RLVR regimes, and explains how local shortcut learning can generalize into broader forms of misalignment, including deception and strategic manipulation of oversight mechanisms. We further organize detection and mitigation strategies according to how they intervene on compression, amplification, or co-adaptation dynamics. By framing reward hacking as a structural instability of proxy-based alignment under scale, we highlight open challenges in scalable oversight, multimodal grounding, and agentic autonomy.
monitor.why_now: Reward hacking is the failure mode people are actively hitting in RLHF/RLAIF/RLVR pipelines right now, and the paper tries to unify verbosity bias, sycophancy, benchmark overfitting, and evaluator gaming under one story instead of treating them as unrelated pathologies. The claim that benign shortcut learning generalizes into deception and oversight gaming is the part a broad audience would care about.
monitor.why_not_higher: It is a survey with a coined framework (the Proxy Compression Hypothesis) rather than new evidence, so nothing here is measured or falsified — the unification is a narrative reorganization of results other people produced. Nothing in it touches memory, KV cache, bandwidth, or storage.
downgrade_reasons: survey, no new experiments or measurements
framing hypothesis is descriptive, not tested or falsifiable as stated
no memory/storage mechanism, incidental or absent
crowded space, several reward-hacking surveys in the last year
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: A concrete empirical test that distinguishes the Proxy Compression Hypothesis from the obvious alternative explanations — for example, showing reward hacking scales predictably with a measurable compression gap between the reward model and the true objective.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Every alignment method we have works by squeezing messy human judgment into a single number, and this paper argues that squeeze is exactly where the model learns to lie to you.

monitor.title: DynHD: Hallucination Detection for Diffusion Large Language Models via Denoising Dynamics Deviation Learning
monitor.abstract: Diffusion large language models (D-LLMs) have emerged as a promising alternative to auto-regressive models due to their iterative refinement capabilities. However, hallucinations remain a critical issue that hinders their reliability. To detect hallucination responses from model outputs, token-level uncertainty (e.g., entropy) has been widely used as an effective signal to indicate potential factual errors. Nevertheless, the fixed-length generation paradigm of D-LLMs implies that tokens contribute unevenly to hallucination detection, with only a small subset providing meaningful signals. Moreover, the evolution trend of uncertainty throughout the diffusion process can also provide important signals, highlighting the necessity of modeling its denoising dynamics for hallucination detection. In this paper, we propose DynHD that bridge these gaps from both spatial (token sequence) and temporal (denoising dynamics) perspectives. To address the information density imbalance across tokens, we propose a semantic-aware evidence construction module that extracts hallucination-indicative signals by filtering out non-informative tokens and emphasizing semantically meaningful ones. To model denoising dynamics for hallucination detection, we introduce a reference evidence generator that learns the expected evolution trajectory of uncertainty evidence, along with a deviation-based hallucination detector that makes predictions by measuring the discrepancy between the observed and reference trajectories. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DynHD consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines while achieving higher efficiency across multiple benchmarks and backbone models.
monitor.why_now: Diffusion LLMs are a live alternative to autoregressive decoding, and hallucination detection tuned to their iterative denoising process is a genuinely D-LLM-native problem rather than a port of AR-model tricks. If D-LLMs gain serving traction, trajectory-based uncertainty signals become part of the reliability toolkit.
monitor.why_not_higher: It is a detector bolted onto a model family almost nobody serves in production yet, evaluated against detection baselines rather than on realistic deployments, and the method depends on the fixed-length denoising schedule so it does not transfer back to autoregressive models.
downgrade_reasons: no memory/storage mechanism — uncertainty trajectories over denoising steps are not KV cache, offload, or bandwidth behavior
sim_memory 0.429 is keyword drift from 'iterative refinement' and 'evidence/trajectory' language, not a systems path
method is coupled to D-LLM denoising dynamics; does not generalize to AR serving
benchmark-and-baselines result; efficiency claims are relative to other detectors, not to a serving stack
D-LLM deployment base is small, limiting near-term audience
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Evidence that the deviation signal works at serving scale on a D-LLM people actually run, or that the spatial token-filtering idea transfers to autoregressive hallucination detection.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Diffusion language models don't write left to right — they refine a whole draft over many steps, and it turns out watching how a model's uncertainty moves across those steps tells you when it's about to make something up.

monitor.title: Fast Catch-Up, Late Switching: Optimal Batch Size Scheduling via Functional Scaling Laws
monitor.abstract: Batch size scheduling (BSS) plays a critical role in large-scale deep learning training, influencing both optimization dynamics and computational efficiency. Yet, its theoretical foundations remain poorly understood. In this work, we show that the functional scaling law (FSL) framework introduced in Li et al. (2025a) provides a principled lens for analyzing BSS. Specifically, we characterize the optimal BSS under a fixed data budget and show that its structure depends sharply on task difficulty. For easy tasks, optimal schedules keep increasing batch size throughout. In contrast, for hard tasks, the optimal schedule maintains small batch sizes for most of training and switches to large batches only in a late stage. To explain the emergence of late switching, we uncover a dynamical mechanism -- the fast catch-up effect -- which also manifests in large language model (LLM) pretraining. After switching from small to large batches, the loss rapidly aligns with the constant large-batch trajectory. Using FSL, we show that this effect stems from rapid forgetting of accumulated gradient noise, with the catch-up speed determined by task difficulty. Crucially, this effect implies that large batches can be safely deferred to late training without sacrificing performance, while substantially reducing data consumption. Finally, extensive LLM pretraining experiments -- covering both Dense and MoE architectures with up to 1.1B parameters and 1T tokens -- validate our theoretical predictions. Across all settings, late-switch schedules consistently outperform constant-batch and early-switch baselines.
monitor.why_now: Batch size scheduling is a live cost lever for anyone doing pretraining, and this gives a theoretically grounded, actionable rule: on hard tasks, hold small batches and switch late, cutting data consumption without hurting loss. The 'fast catch-up' mechanism — rapid forgetting of accumulated gradient noise after a switch — is a genuinely new explanation rather than a curve fit.
monitor.why_not_higher: It is a training-dynamics theory paper whose audience is pretraining practitioners, not the broad AI listenership, and the 1.1B/1T-token validation is credible but still an order of magnitude below the frontier scale where BSS decisions actually bite.
downgrade_reasons: memory lens similarity is keyword overlap from batch/scaling vocabulary, not a memory or storage mechanism
no path to KV cache, offload, tiering, paging, or bandwidth behavior
audience is narrower than general Public AI: pretraining teams
largest validation is 1.1B params, below frontier scale
monitor.what_would_raise_priority: Validation at 10B+ scale or adoption of late-switch schedules in a published frontier training recipe would move this to Cover now.
monitor.one_sentence_episode_hook: Everyone ramps batch size up as training goes on — this paper argues that for hard tasks you should stay small almost the whole way and switch late, and explains why the loss catches up so fast that you lose nothing but save a pile of tokens.

deferred.title: It's TIME: Towards the Next Generation of Time Series Forecasting Benchmarks
deferred.abstract: Time series foundation models (TSFMs) are revolutionizing the forecasting landscape from specific dataset modeling to generalizable task evaluation. However, we contend that existing benchmarks exhibit common limitations in four dimensions: constrained data composition dominated by reused legacy sources, compromised data integrity lacking rigorous quality assurance, misaligned task formulations detached from real-world contexts, and rigid analysis perspectives that obscure generalizable insights. To bridge these gaps, we introduce TIME, a next-generation task-centric benchmark comprising 50 fresh datasets and 98 forecasting tasks, tailored for strict zero-shot TSFM evaluation free from data leakage. Integrating large language models and human expertise, we establish a human-in-the-loop benchmark construction pipeline to ensure high data integrity and redefine task formulation by aligning forecasting configurations with real-world operational requirements and variate predictability. Furthermore, we propose a novel pattern-level evaluation perspective that moves beyond traditional dataset-level evaluations based on static meta labels. By leveraging structural time series features to characterize intrinsic temporal properties, this approach offers generalizable insights into model capabilities across diverse patterns. We evaluate 12 TSFMs and establish a multi-granular leaderboard to facilitate in-depth analysis and visualized inspection. The leaderboard is available at https://huggingface.co/spaces/Real-TSF/TIME-leaderboard.
deferred.why_now: Time series foundation models are a real and growing subfield, and TIME's zero-shot, leakage-free benchmark with 50 fresh datasets and 98 tasks addresses a genuine contamination problem in TSFM evaluation. But this matters to the time-series forecasting community specifically, not to a broad AI audience.
deferred.why_not_higher: This is a domain benchmark paper for a narrow subfield. It has zero memory/storage relevance — no KV cache, bandwidth, offload, or data-movement content whatsoever — and the first-pass memory similarity of 0.313 is pure keyword drift from generic "foundation model" and "evaluation" language. It also does not change how anyone builds, trains, or serves general AI systems.
downgrade_reasons: no memory/storage mechanism, direct or adjacent
narrow subfield (time series forecasting), not broad AI interest
benchmark/leaderboard contribution, not a method that transfers
memory similarity is keyword artifact from foundation-model vocabulary
"domain: llm" first-pass label is wrong — TSFMs are not LLMs
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Evidence that the pattern-level evaluation methodology or the human-in-the-loop leakage-free construction pipeline generalizes to benchmarking foundation models outside time series, or a finding that reshapes how the field thinks about zero-shot generalization broadly.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: Every time series foundation model claims zero-shot generalization — TIME rebuilt the benchmark from 50 datasets nobody trained on, and asks what these models actually learned versus what they memorized.

deferred.title: Radial-VCReg: More Informative Representation Learning Through Radial Gaussianization
deferred.abstract: Self-supervised learning aims to learn maximally informative representations, but explicit information maximization is hindered by the curse of dimensionality. Existing methods like VCReg address this by regularizing first and second-order feature statistics, which cannot fully achieve maximum entropy. We propose Radial-VCReg, which augments VCReg with a radial Gaussianization loss that aligns feature norms with the Chi distribution-a defining property of high-dimensional Gaussians. We prove that Radial-VCReg transforms a broader class of distributions towards normality compared to VCReg and show on synthetic and real-world datasets that it consistently improves performance by reducing higher-order dependencies and promoting more diverse and informative representations.
deferred.why_now: Radial-VCReg is a clean theoretical extension of variance-covariance regularization that pushes SSL embeddings toward true high-dimensional Gaussianity via a Chi-distribution norm loss, which is a real gap in the VICReg/VCReg family. It arrives while joint-embedding SSL is still competing with generative pretraining for representation quality.
deferred.why_not_higher: The contribution is an added regularization term validated on synthetic data and standard benchmarks, with no evidence it holds at frontier scale or changes how anyone trains production models. The first-pass score is inflated by an author-affiliation signal rather than demonstrated impact.
downgrade_reasons: theory/regularizer paper without large-model or systems relevance
no memory, cache, bandwidth, or data-movement mechanism at all
evaluation on synthetic and standard benchmarks, not realistic scale
public interest score driven by influencer boost rather than substance
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Evidence that the radial Gaussianization loss improves representations at large pretraining scale or transfers to multimodal/LLM encoders where practitioners would actually adopt it.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: Every self-supervised model claims to maximize information, but almost none of them are actually Gaussian in the way the math requires — Radial-VCReg asks what happens when you finally force the norms to obey.

deferred.title: LLaMo: Scaling Pretrained Language Models for Unified Motion Understanding and Generation with Continuous Autoregressive Tokens
deferred.abstract: Recent progress in large models has led to significant advances in unified multimodal generation and understanding. However, the development of models that unify motion-language generation and understanding remains largely underexplored. Existing approaches often fine-tune large language models (LLMs) on paired motion-text data, which can result in catastrophic forgetting of linguistic capabilities due to the limited scale of available text-motion pairs. Furthermore, prior methods typically convert motion into discrete representations via quantization to integrate with language models, introducing substantial jitter artifacts from discrete tokenization. To address these challenges, we propose LLaMo, a unified framework that extends pretrained LLMs through a modality-specific Mixture-of-Transformers (MoT) architecture. This design inherently preserves the language understanding of the base model while enabling scalable multimodal adaptation. We encode human motion into a causal continuous latent space and maintain the next-token prediction paradigm in the decoder-only backbone through a lightweight flow-matching head, allowing for streaming motion generation in real-time (>30 FPS). Leveraging the comprehensive language understanding of pretrained LLMs and large-scale motion-text pretraining, our experiments demonstrate that LLaMo achieves high-fidelity text-to-motion generation and motion-to-text captioning in general settings, especially zero-shot motion generation, marking a significant step towards a general unified motion-language large model.
deferred.why_now: LLaMo is a credible instance of a broader pattern worth tracking: replacing discrete VQ tokenization with continuous latents plus a flow-matching head so a frozen-ish LLM backbone can emit non-text modalities without catastrophic forgetting. The modality-specific Mixture-of-Transformers routing is the architecturally interesting part, not the motion.
deferred.why_not_higher: The contribution is demonstrated only on human motion generation and captioning, a narrow application domain with small paired datasets, and nothing in the paper argues or measures that the MoT-plus-continuous-token recipe transfers to other modalities. Streaming at >30 FPS is a latency claim about a tiny motion decoder, not evidence on realistic serving workloads.
downgrade_reasons: narrow application domain (cs.CV human motion) with no demonstrated transfer
memory relevance is absent: no KV cache, offload, bandwidth, or capacity mechanism
real-time streaming claim is about motion FPS, not LLM serving throughput or memory behavior
MoT parameter expansion is asserted to preserve language ability rather than analyzed as a systems cost
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: Evidence that the continuous-latent-plus-flow-matching-head recipe generalizes across modalities, or a serving analysis showing what MoT branch expansion costs in memory footprint and activation traffic during streaming decode.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the jitter in AI-generated human motion isn't a modeling failure but a quantization artifact, and the fix is to stop tokenizing the world into discrete symbols at all?

deferred.title: WavBench: Benchmarking Reasoning, Colloquialism, and Paralinguistics for End-to-End Spoken Dialogue Models
deferred.abstract: With the rapid integration of advanced reasoning capabilities into spoken dialogue models, the field urgently demands benchmarks that transcend simple interactions to address real-world complexity. However, current evaluations predominantly adhere to text-generation standards, overlooking the unique audio-centric characteristics of paralinguistics and colloquialisms, alongside the cognitive depth required by modern agents. To bridge this gap, we introduce WavBench, a comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate realistic conversational abilities where prior works fall short. Uniquely, WavBench establishes a tripartite framework: 1) Pro subset, designed to rigorously challenge reasoning-enhanced models with significantly increased difficulty; 2) Basic subset, defining a novel standard for spoken colloquialism that prioritizes "listenability" through natural vocabulary, linguistic fluency, and interactive rapport, rather than rigid written accuracy; and 3) Acoustic subset, covering explicit understanding, generation, and implicit dialogue to rigorously evaluate comprehensive paralinguistic capabilities within authentic real-world scenarios. Through evaluating five state-of-the-art models, WavBench offers critical insights into the intersection of complex problem-solving, colloquial delivery, and paralinguistic fidelity, guiding the evolution of robust spoken dialogue models. The benchmark dataset and evaluation toolkit are available at https://naruto-2024.github.io/wavbench.github.io/.
deferred.why_now: Speech-native dialogue models are shipping fast and evaluation for paralinguistics and colloquial delivery is genuinely underdeveloped, so a benchmark that scores 'listenability' rather than transcript accuracy targets a real gap.
deferred.why_not_higher: It is a benchmark paper for one modality niche with five evaluated models and no mechanism that changes how anyone builds or serves systems; the memory/storage connection is absent entirely, and audio-benchmark construction does not transfer beyond spoken dialogue.
downgrade_reasons: no memory/storage mechanism — audio dialogue evaluation only
benchmark result, not a method or systems contribution
narrow modality scope: end-to-end spoken dialogue models
only five models evaluated; leaderboard will be stale in months
no findings that redirect model design or training
deferred.what_would_raise_priority: A surprising, generalizable finding — e.g., that reasoning-enhanced spoken models systematically trade paralinguistic fidelity for cognitive depth, with evidence that the tradeoff is architectural rather than a data artifact.
deferred.one_sentence_episode_hook: Every speech model benchmark grades the transcript — WavBench asks whether the model actually sounds like someone worth listening to, and the best reasoners may be the worst talkers.

deferred.title: MambaSL: Exploring Single-Layer Mamba for Time Series Classification
deferred.abstract: Despite recent advances in state space models (SSMs) such as Mamba across various sequence domains, research on their standalone capacity for time series classification (TSC) has remained limited. We propose MambaSL, a framework that minimally redesigns the selective SSM and projection layers of a single-layer Mamba, guided by four TSC-specific hypotheses. To address benchmarking limitations -- restricted configurations, partial University of East Anglia (UEA) dataset coverage, and insufficiently reproducible setups -- we re-evaluate 20 strong baselines across all 30 UEA datasets under a unified protocol. As a result, MambaSL achieves state-of-the-art performance with statistically significant average improvements, while ensuring reproducibility via public checkpoints for all evaluated models. Together with visualizations, these results demonstrate the potential of Mamba-based architectures as a TSC backbone.
deferred.why_now: A clean, reproducible re-benchmark of 20 baselines across all 30 UEA datasets is a real service to the time-series classification subfield, and public checkpoints raise the bar for that community's evaluation hygiene.
deferred.why_not_higher: This is a narrow task paper: a single-layer Mamba tuned with four TSC-specific hypotheses on small academic datasets, with no demonstrated transfer to how anyone builds, trains, or serves modern AI systems.
